parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game centers on trading red and blue chips, which have different private values for each player, while green chips are common-value and therefore strategically simple: everyone values green at $0.50. The key private-value asymmetries are: buffalo values blue highly and red very little (BLUE 0.70, RED 0.10), bear values both blue and red fairly highly (BLUE 1.00, RED 0.60), and goose values both blue and red very little (BLUE 0.10, RED 0.10). Because green is fixed at $0.50 for all, it serves as a natural medium of exchange or safe reference asset, whereas red and blue are the main sources of potential gains from trade. From an efficiency standpoint, the ideal allocation would send most blue chips to bear (the highest blue value) and reasonably many blue chips to buffalo, and send surplus red away from goose and buffalo (who value red only $0.10) toward bear (who values red at $0.60), with goose often acting as a low-value holder willing to trade away blue or red for green. However, the players do not know each other’s exact valuations; they only see their own calculator and observed trading behavior, so they must infer who likes which chip colors by what offers and acceptances appear. Buffalo is numerate, reflective, and highly cooperative; she is likely to look for “mutually good” trades and might use green as a safe currency, but she is also uncertainty-averse and unwilling to sacrifice clear personal value for purely speculative gains. Bear has the strongest marginal value for blue and decent value for red; she may be motivated to accumulate blue and red but faces uncertainty about which partner will respond, so she needs to design proposals that will be accepted by at least one counterpart and still leave her a positive surplus. Goose has low private values for red and blue and the same value for green as others; she is a natural seller of red and blue if she can gain extra green, but being older and reasonably numerate, she may hesitate to make trades that feel risky or complex. Strategic uncertainty comes from three main sources: (1) incomplete information about others’ values; (2) simultaneous acceptance with random partner selection if more than one player accepts; and (3) the limited three-round horizon with one proposal per player each round, which pressures players to make reasonably attractive offers early rather than waiting for perfect information. Overall, the likely pattern is that buffalo and bear try to move away from low-value chips toward higher-value ones (especially blue), while goose tends to sell away red or blue for green when the personal calculator shows a gain. Many low-margin offers will be declined, but some simple, clearly positive-sum trades—especially ones that swap red for green or blue for green in goose’s favor while helping bear or buffalo rebalance—are likely to be accepted.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 chips of each color and have no information about others’ private valuations beyond the public range. Proposers in this round are likely to experiment with simple, easy-to-understand offers that test market interest: small quantities, involving green as a reference asset, and plainly beneficial from their own perspective. Because no one has yet observed any acceptances or rejections, beliefs about others’ preferences are very diffuse. Buffalo, with strong numeracy and high cooperation orientation, may start by gently probing whether others are willing to part with blue or green at reasonable prices. Bear may already want to acquire blue or red but will be cautious not to post obviously one-sided deals that would surely be rejected. Goose, with low marginal values for red and blue, will be open to any offer that turns those into extra green or, less likely, into blue if she starts to suspect others like blue more. In this first round, we expect mostly conservative proposals with moderate markups and a high rate of rejection while everyone calibrates what might be acceptable.

By the start of round 1, the history from round 0 has revealed some structure: goose accepted trading 2 BLUE for 2 GREEN, signaling that goose’s valuation of blue is likely low relative to green, and bear accepted a trade exchanging 3 RED for 2 GREEN, signaling that bear values red relatively highly compared with green. Buffalo now holds fewer green and more blue; goose holds fewer blue and red but more green; bear holds more red and fewer green. These observed moves give each player partial information: goose looks like a seller of red and blue, while bear looks like a buyer of red at a favorable rate. Buffalo, having gained blue cheaply, may become more confident and try to continue trading green for blue when possible but must now manage her reduced green inventory. Bear, having already bought red at a good implicit price, might now experiment with buying blue—though she does not yet know that her blue valuation is the highest in the group—or with selling some red back to buffalo if it can be turned into green or blue profitably. Goose, richer in green and poorer in red/blue, is now positioned as a reasonably liquid buyer if she sees an opportunity to convert some green into red or blue at favorable rates, though her intrinsic valuations for red and blue are still low. Proposals in this round will likely be more targeted: small, value-positive offers that exploit the newly inferred roles (bear as red buyer, goose as red/blue seller, buffalo as blue buyer).

Round 2 is the final opportunity for trading, so players face an endgame environment: they can ignore any dynamic or reputational concerns and focus purely on immediate surplus. By this point, the observed history strongly suggests that goose is consistently happy to give up red or blue for green at modestly unfavorable rates, while bear is willing to acquire red and possibly blue when the calculator shows a clear gain, and buffalo reliably accepts trades that convert red or blue into blue or green when they are positive for her. Buffalo now likely holds a portfolio tilted further toward blue and away from green, bear holds more red and perhaps some additional blue, and goose holds more green but fewer red and blue. Given the impending end of the game, proposals now can be more aggressive but still must show strictly positive surplus to the counterparty; lowball offers will simply be rejected. We expect relatively simple trades, often using green as the payment medium, that exploit known value asymmetries: buffalo or bear offering red to goose for green, bear offering red for blue or vice versa, and goose possibly making one final attempt to convert low-value red or blue into green. Since there is no future beyond this round, players who see a sure small gain will likely accept rather than wait for a better, nonexistent future opportunity.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players value RED at $1 and GREEN at $0.50, but differ on BLUE: otter values BLUE at $0.40, peacock at $0.50, and cat at $0.70. This creates a clear pattern: cat has the highest BLUE value and equal RED value, so she is especially willing to give up RED or GREEN for BLUE; otter has the lowest BLUE value, so she is most willing to sell BLUE and buy RED; peacock sits in the middle and is close to indifferent on many exchanges of BLUE versus GREEN, but still likes RED the most. Because RED is uniformly the highest-valued color and GREEN has a fixed $0.50 common value, the main surplus opportunities involve moving BLUE from otter and peacock toward cat, and moving some RED from cat and peacock toward otter (and sometimes toward cat if she can cheaply buy extra RED). However, each player only knows their own values and only knows that the public range for RED and BLUE is $0.10–$1.00, so they have substantial uncertainty about how much others value each color. That uncertainty pushes them toward small, evidently profitable trades that seem likely to be acceptable to at least one partner, rather than extreme, highly demanding offers. The simultaneous and private acceptance rule plus random tie-breaking among multiple acceptors means senders can’t target a specific partner; they must offer terms that are attractive enough for someone to bite while recognizing they might accidentally trade with the higher-value counterparty. Over three short rounds with one proposal per player per round, they have limited time to learn about others’ valuations from what is accepted or rejected. Likely dynamics: in early turns, players test simple, near-fair or slightly favorable ratios (for example, swapping 1 RED for 2 BLUE or 1 RED for 2 GREEN) that are guaranteed gains from their own perspective but plausibly still attractive for at least one opponent. Rejections gradually reveal that others also value RED highly and likely value BLUE more than the proposer hoped, especially once they see cat repeatedly willing to give up RED or GREEN to get BLUE. The game is therefore characterized by cautious, incremental proposals, competition between peacock and cat to acquire BLUE from otter at favorable terms, and occasional mutually beneficial RED-for-BLUE or RED-for-GREEN trades that leave all realized partners with positive surplus. Because all three have relatively high uncertainty aversion, they avoid proposals that risk obvious losses even with a small chance of acceptance, focusing instead on clearly positive-surplus options, which limits but does not eliminate overall efficiency.

rounds.round_explanation: In the first round, no one has observed any behavior yet, so beliefs about others’ chip values are diffuse. Each player knows RED is likely valuable to others, but is unsure how much others care about BLUE relative to GREEN. Cautious, low-stakes proposals are expected as they probe the market. Otter, with the lowest BLUE value, is inclined to sell BLUE or acquire RED cheaply, but she doesn’t yet know that cat values BLUE very highly. Peacock, with moderate BLUE value and very high uncertainty aversion, prefers trades that do not look exploitative and that keep her clearly in the black. Cat, who values BLUE the most, will quickly test whether she can cheaply buy BLUE from others using RED or GREEN; early acceptances or rejections will shape her sense of how competitive the market for BLUE is.

Going into round 1, everyone has seen that otter was unwilling to trade 2 BLUE for 1 RED, which hints she values BLUE non-trivially, and that peacock could not get anyone to give up BLUE for GREEN at a 1:1 ratio. Crucially, cat successfully bought BLUE from otter at a 1:1 GREEN-for-BLUE rate, revealing to otter that someone is willing to pay at least $0.50-equivalent per BLUE in terms of GREEN. Otter now knows that at least one player values BLUE more than she does, encouraging her to continue selling BLUE when she can get GREEN or perhaps RED at friendly terms. Cat now knows that at least one opponent (otter) finds a 1 GREEN for 1 BLUE trade acceptable, confirming her comparative advantage in BLUE. Peacock updates that GREEN is not attractive currency for BLUE at 1:1—she will need to either sweeten her offers with RED or adjust which color she is targeting. In this round, proposals become slightly more targeted as each player tries to consolidate positions: otter tries to improve her overall value by turning BLUE into RED, peacock tentatively tests exchanging RED for BLUE at ratios that still look good to her, and cat continues to push to buy BLUE while selling off GREEN or a little RED.

By the final round, the trade history suggests that both otter and peacock will part with BLUE for sufficiently generous amounts of GREEN or for RED, while cat is consistently eager to acquire BLUE and is willing to pay with GREEN and occasionally RED. Otter has already sold some BLUE to both cat and peacock at terms that improved her total value, reinforcing her role as a BLUE supplier. Peacock has learned that paying 1 RED for 2 BLUE can be acceptable to at least one counterpart and that she can then resell BLUE to cat for GREEN at favorable rates, effectively arbitraging between the two. Cat understands that she is often the high-valuation buyer for BLUE and that the others will not give her extremely favorable prices; she instead looks for modest but still profitable trades given her $0.70 per BLUE. In this last round, players recognize that there is no future to preserve relationships or signal; they focus on any remaining positive-surplus opportunities. Proposals become slightly more aggressive but must still be clearly value-increasing to get accepted in a single shot, given everyone’s aversion to making a loss on the calculator.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features symmetric initial endowments (10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN each) but asymmetric private values for RED and BLUE. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 to everyone, and there is no time discounting or carryover across games. Because each player sees only their own payoff calculator, they know exactly how any proposed trade affects their own payoff but do not know the others’ dollar values and must infer them from observed behavior.

Value profile and core incentives:
- badger-red-5032: RED = $0.80, BLUE = $0.80, GREEN = $0.50. Badger values both RED and BLUE more than GREEN. They should be willing to give up GREEN to get either RED or BLUE, and be reluctant to part with RED/BLUE unless they receive at least as much value back.
- camel-yellow-5577: RED = $0.60, BLUE = $0.40, GREEN = $0.50. Camel likes RED more than GREEN, and GREEN more than BLUE. Camel will want to convert BLUE into RED or GREEN, and might trade away some GREEN for RED if the rate is favorable, but will be less willing to give away RED.
- squid-yellow-9545: RED = $0.20, BLUE = $0.60, GREEN = $0.50. Squid strongly prefers BLUE and GREEN to RED. Squid will want to trade away RED to get BLUE or GREEN and will hesitate to part with BLUE unless they get even more value back, or unless they see a very favorable multi-step opportunity.

From a social-surplus perspective, the highest uses are: RED in the hands of Badger or Camel (0.8 and 0.6) rather than Squid (0.2), BLUE in the hands of Badger or Squid (0.8 and 0.6) rather than Camel (0.4), and GREEN is symmetric at 0.5. Mutual gains exist when, for example, Squid trades low-value RED for GREEN or BLUE, Camel trades low-value BLUE for GREEN or RED, and Badger trades GREEN for either RED or BLUE. However, trades must make both sides individually better off at their private values, and proposers cannot target a specific recipient if multiple players accept. This randomization risk discourages proposals that are good with one counterpart but bad with another.

Strategically, each proposer faces:
1) Private-value uncertainty: they only see their own surplus calculator, so they must infer others’ valuations from what trades they accept or reject. Early in the game, they may explore with modest offers that seem broadly attractive to at least one counterpart.
2) Recipient-selection uncertainty: if a proposal is good for multiple players, any of them may accept and the recipient is randomly chosen. This encourages designing offers that are acceptable to whatever counterpart ends up matched, or sufficiently beneficial that even the worst-case counterpart acceptance still yields a gain.
3) Three-round horizon: with only three rounds and one proposal per player per round, there is limited time to learn or coordinate on complex trades. Players are likely to focus on straightforward, clearly profitable one-step trades (e.g., trading away their least-valued color for a more-valued color) instead of speculative sequences.

Given the personas, all three players are moderately to highly cooperative but still payoff-maximizing. Camel and Squid display high cognitive and financial literacy, so they are likely to calculate net gains precisely and will rarely accept negative-surplus trades. Badger is somewhat less numerate but still reasonably capable, and with high cooperation orientation may occasionally offer slightly generous terms to increase the chance of acceptance, without sacrificing much value. Across the three rounds, we expect a pattern of early cautious probing, followed by more targeted trades as players infer who likes which colors. Because Squid values RED so little, they are the natural buyer of BLUE and GREEN and seller of RED; Camel is a natural buyer of RED and seller of BLUE; Badger is a natural buyer of both RED and BLUE and seller of GREEN. Many win–win trades involve moving RED away from Squid and BLUE away from Camel toward Badger, often mediated through GREEN.

The forecast below reconstructs one plausible full trajectory of proposals and accept/reject decisions, assuming each player looks for clearly positive expected value, simple proposals, and avoids offers where the random recipient risk could make the trade negative for them.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, all players start with identical 10–10–10 holdings but unknown private values of others. We expect exploratory but self-beneficial offers. Badger and Camel, both valuing RED more than BLUE or GREEN, will likely try to acquire RED using colors they value less. Squid, who values RED least and BLUE most, will be inclined to offload RED in exchange for BLUE or GREEN. Each proposer must also consider that either of the two non-senders might accept, so they focus on trades that look plausibly attractive to at least one counterpart without obviously sacrificing value.

By round 1, some information has been revealed. From round 0: Badger learned that a relatively conservative GREEN-for-RED offer did not appeal, hinting that others may not strongly prefer GREEN to RED. Camel and Squid executed a BLUE-for-RED trade, revealing: (i) Squid is very willing to exchange RED for BLUE, and (ii) Camel is happy to give up BLUE for RED. Then Squid traded RED for GREEN with Camel, showing that Camel also accepts RED-for-GREEN at a favorable rate and that Squid is content to convert more RED into GREEN. By now, everyone can infer that Squid dislikes RED and that Camel dislikes BLUE, while Camel likely likes RED. Badger still has many GREEN chips and sees that both others have been willing to move RED; Camel now holds substantial RED and somewhat reduced BLUE and GREEN; Squid has shed RED in favor of BLUE and GREEN. In this round, proposers move from pure exploration to more targeted beneficial trades, often using GREEN as the mediating asset.

By the final round, the trading pattern is quite clear: Squid consistently trades away RED and is eager for BLUE and GREEN; Camel trades away BLUE and is comfortable acquiring RED or GREEN; Badger trades away GREEN to gain higher-valued colored chips. Everyone now has strong beliefs about the others’ rankings of colors, even if not their exact dollar values. With only one proposal turn left each, players prioritize high-certainty, positive-surplus trades that subtly rebalance portfolios toward their favorite colors. They avoid risky proposals that might end up matched with the "wrong" counterparty due to random recipient selection, instead focusing on offers that are acceptable for at least one known compatible partner and still give them a solid gain.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This 3-player, 3-round bargaining game features common-value green chips ($0.50 for everyone) and privately valued red/blue chips. Each player starts with 10 of each color. From an analyst perspective, the key force is that red and blue have very different private values, so mutually beneficial trades typically involve moving chips toward those who value them more, while moving away chips that are relatively less valuable to that player.

Private values:
- dog-blue-6080 (Dog): RED $1.00 (high), BLUE $0.20 (low), GREEN $0.50.
- butterfly-blue-3807 (Butterfly): BLUE $0.90 (high), RED $0.60 (medium), GREEN $0.50.
- ram-purple-9443 (Ram): RED $0.40 (low), BLUE $0.10 (very low), GREEN $0.50.

Efficient patterns (from the analyst’s view) would be:
- RED flows mainly toward Dog (highest red value) and somewhat toward Butterfly (medium); away from Ram (lowest).
- BLUE flows toward Butterfly (highest blue value); away from Dog and especially Ram.
- GREEN is neutral since everyone values it equally; it mainly serves as a flexible medium for balancing trades or as a perceived safe asset.

But each player only sees their own valuations and observes others’ proposals and acceptances, using them to infer who may value which color. Early in the game, uncertainty about others’ values is high; players often start with conservative, mildly positive-surplus trades that are easy to accept. The surplus calculator makes clear to each player whether a given trade is individually profitable, so accepted trades almost always yield non-negative private surplus for both parties. Proposers must also reckon with the random tie-breaking if multiple recipients accept: any proposal that is attractive to more than one partner risks executing with the one the proposer would least prefer, so players often structure offers to be clearly more appealing to a specific partner.

Given the personas, Butterfly and Ram are relatively analytic and cooperative, likely to calculate and pursue modest but clear gains, while Dog is empathetic but more uncertainty-averse and slightly weaker on numeracy, likely to start with simple, easy-to-understand offers and avoid highly risky or complex terms. Because everyone has three proposal turns, we expect some experimentation in early rounds, followed by more targeted offers once players infer that Butterfly strongly values blue, Dog strongly values red, and Ram is relatively weak on both red and blue and more oriented toward safe green holdings. Over three rounds, a likely trajectory is: (1) initial feeler trades revealing who likes which chips, (2) mid-game attempts to exploit perceived comparative advantages (e.g., swapping Dog’s blue for others’ red, or Butterfly’s red/green for others’ blue), and (3) end-game tightening, where players become pickier and only accept clearly positive and strategically aligned trades as the opportunity to adjust portfolios runs out.

rounds.round_explanation: Round 0 is the exploration phase. No one yet knows others’ valuations, so players will likely try simple, symmetrical-looking trades that offer modest private gains and test counterpart preferences. Observed acceptances and rejections here will start to signal which colors each counterpart might particularly value, especially around blue and red.

By Round 1, some information is revealed: Butterfly has shown willingness to trade for blue (and accept blue cheaply from Ram), suggesting Butterfly values blue highly. Ram has shown a readiness to dump blue for green, revealing very low blue valuation and a preference for safe green. Dog’s initial successful trade with Butterfly for blue in exchange for green hints that Dog is at least willing to hold extra blue if the terms look favorable. Now players start making more targeted proposals: Butterfly will aim to accumulate blue more aggressively; Ram will continue to convert unwanted blue into green; Dog will try to protect high-value red and maybe offload low-value blue when possible.

Entering the final round, each player has clearer beliefs: Butterfly likely sees both Dog and Ram as relatively willing to part with blue under the right terms, and may suspect Dog values red highly. Ram recognizes that both others may give up green or red to adjust blue holdings, and will continue to trade away low-value colored chips for green or for modest surplus. Dog understands that others are eager for blue and may try to use remaining blue as a bargaining chip to secure more red or green. Because this is the last opportunity to improve portfolios, players are more selective: they propose trades with clear positive expected surplus and only accept trades that leave them strictly better off in value terms.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game centers on trading red and blue chips, which have different private values to each participant, while green chips are common-value and thus provide a natural, low-risk medium of exchange. Each player begins symmetric in chip counts but asymmetric in values: Alligator values blue moderately and red least; Lion values blue very highly and red moderately; Iguana values red very highly and blue least. Green is worth the same ($0.50) to everyone. This structure creates classic gains-from-trade opportunities: Lion wants to acquire blue (especially from Alligator, who values it less than Lion does), and Iguana wants to acquire red (especially from Alligator and Lion, who value it less than Iguana does). However, because each player only knows their own valuations, they must infer others’ preferences from observed proposals and acceptances. The simultaneous, private accept/decline decisions plus random partner selection when multiple players accept introduce strategic uncertainty: proposers cannot target a partner, and responders may worry about being undercut by the third player. Rational players will tend to propose trades that are clearly positive for themselves and plausibly positive for at least one counterpart, often using small, safe trades early to probe others’ valuations. Over the three rounds, players update beliefs about who values which color and adjust offers accordingly. The green chip’s known value acts as a coordination anchor: offering or requesting green can signal how expensive or cheap the proposer perceives the other colors to be. With only nine total proposal turns and no future continuation, players face mild time pressure to secure at least some surplus-enhancing trades before the game ends, but they also avoid clearly negative or extremely risky proposals, especially early, given uncertainty aversion and the random matching when multiple players accept.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, all players share only the public information about chip ranges and green’s common value. They do not yet know each other’s exact red/blue valuations. Round 0 is therefore largely exploratory: proposers are likely to make simple, low-risk offers that are slightly favorable to themselves but look potentially acceptable to at least one counterpart. Green is a natural probe currency, since everyone knows it is worth $0.50 to all. Early trades that move small quantities of red or blue for green can reveal who is keen to buy which color, informing more targeted proposals later.

After Round 0, some information has been revealed: Alligator showed willingness to give up blue for green, suggesting blue is not extremely precious to them; Lion paid green for blue, indicating a relatively high private value of blue; and Iguana successfully bought red with green, hinting that red is especially valuable to Iguana. Players now have better (though still imperfect) beliefs about who values what. In Round 1, proposers are more inclined to design trades that target these inferred preferences: Lion as a blue buyer, Iguana as a red buyer, and Alligator as a flexible trader using green as a buffer. We should see somewhat more aggressive but still mutually beneficial proposals, often pairing red/blue against green to keep counterpart risk low.

By Round 2, trade history has made value patterns clearer: Lion persistently acquires blue; Iguana persistently acquires red; Alligator is willing to part with blue for green and sometimes trade blue for red if compensated. With only three proposals left, players become more opportunistic, looking for any remaining positive-value swaps to fine-tune their portfolios before the game ends. They will avoid trades that meaningfully cut into their strongest holdings (Lion’s blue, Iguana’s red) unless the compensation is substantial. At this stage, medium-sized, clearly positive deals are more likely than tiny probes or wildly speculative offers, since there is no future round in which to exploit new information.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features differentiated private values on red and blue chips and a common value on green chips. Each player starts symmetric in quantities (10 of each color) but asymmetric in valuations: whale-red-86 values red highly ($1.00) and blue moderately ($0.40); dolphin-orange-3122 values blue highly ($0.80) and red moderately ($0.60); duck-orange-4971 values red highly ($1.00) and blue at $0.60. All value green at $0.50.

The main surplus opportunities are cross-type trades converting lower-valued chips into higher-valued ones for both sides. For example, whale and dolphin can both gain from trades where whale gives blue for dolphin's red, and dolphin gives red for whale's blue. Similarly, dolphin is willing to trade red to duck for blue, and duck is willing to trade blue to dolphin for red, within appropriate ratios. However, the random recipient rule when multiple players accept means proposers cannot target a specific partner if both counter-parties like the same side of the deal. This strategic uncertainty encourages slightly more conservative or differentiated offers that are still acceptable to either counter-party, or sometimes offers designed so that only one counter-party is likely to accept.

Given their backgrounds, all three players are moderately cooperative but individually payoff-maximizing. They have on-screen surplus calculators, so they will rarely accept strictly negative expected-value trades. Because they do not know others’ private values, early-round proposals will also serve as informative signals: what a player offers and asks can reveal relative chip preferences. Over the three rounds, we expect an exploration pattern in early turns (testing rough ratios in red–blue and green–color trades) followed by more targeted, higher-surplus trades once players infer who especially wants red or blue. The finite horizon (3 rounds, 9 total proposal turns) also pressures players not to wait too long for perfect offers; mutually beneficial trades are likely to occur in rounds 1–2 once some learning has happened.

The forecast below reconstructs one plausible complete path of play in which players gradually discover complementarity between dolphin’s high blue value and whale/duck’s high red value, negotiate around the random-recipient risk, and execute a handful of positive-surplus swaps. Where multiple behaviors are plausible, the reconstruction favors simple, clearly value-increasing trades and avoids dominated or obviously negative-value moves, consistent with use of the surplus calculator and moderate numeracy.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, players have no direct evidence of each other’s chip valuations. Proposals are likely to be exploratory, small in size, and symmetric-looking so they appear fair under many possible value profiles. Because everyone knows green is common-value at $0.50, early trades may test red/blue against green or red against blue at near-1:1 ratios. Acceptance patterns in this round will start revealing who especially values red or blue. Players will also begin to internalize the random-recipient risk: if both potential recipients like the same side of a deal, the proposer cannot target one specifically.

By round 1, the executed dolphin–whale trade from round 0 has revealed partial information: dolphin was willing to give up more blue than red (3 blue for 2 red), hinting that dolphin values red less than blue, and whale’s acceptance shows red is not low-value for whale. Duck has not yet traded, but saw that whale accepted red in exchange for blue, and will infer whale likely values red at least as much as blue. Players now begin to refine proposals: dolphin and duck will test red-for-blue rates that yield positive surplus for them, and whale will try to exploit its now-higher red holdings to gain more blue from dolphin while still leaving dolphin better off. The random-recipient issue is salient if both whale and duck want to be on the same profitable side of dolphin’s offers.

Entering the final round, some strong patterns are now observable. Whale and duck have repeatedly taken trades increasing their red relative to blue, revealing high red value. Dolphin has consistently been willing to give red for blue at roughly balanced or slightly favorable rates, revealing relatively higher blue value. With only three proposal turns left, players have limited time to exploit remaining gains from trade. They will prefer simple, clearly positive-surplus offers that align with these inferred preferences. The random-recipient rule still matters, but after multiple trades players now better predict which counterpart is likely to accept which side, making it easier to craft offers that at least one counterpart will take without leaving money on the table.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start symmetric in chip counts (10 of each color) but asymmetric in how much they privately value red and blue. Green is common-value at $0.50 for everyone. Gorilla values red the most ($1.00) and blue moderately ($0.70). Turtle values blue most ($1.00) and red slightly less ($0.90). Zebra values both red and blue relatively low ($0.60 and $0.50). These private values create strong gains-from-trade opportunities, especially where a player with a low value for a color can profitably give it up to someone who values it more, in exchange for a color they themselves value more. However, players only know their own values and see only ranges for others, so they must infer counterpart values from observed offers and acceptances. They also know that only one recipient can be matched if multiple parties accept a proposal: proposers cannot target a specific partner once multiple acceptances occur. The on-screen surplus calculator ensures that no player will voluntarily accept negative-surplus trades, but they may still accept low, positive-surplus trades when they are uncertain whether better opportunities will arise in later rounds. Given the short, 3-round horizon and the fixed proposer order (turtle, gorilla, zebra each round), early turns are likely to feature exploratory, relatively conservative offers with small but positive surplus for the proposer against most plausible counterpart values. As information accumulates from who accepts what, proposals can be sharpened: high-value holders (turtle for blue, gorilla for red) will try to accumulate their favored colors from low-value holders (especially zebra), while low-value holders will try to liquidate low-value colors for anything that pays more in their own valuation. Because zebra’s values are weakest for both red and blue, zebra is the most natural supplier of surplus to turtle and gorilla, but zebra’s very high uncertainty aversion suggests she will also happily accept modest but safe gains when they appear. Cooperative orientations and empathy in all three profiles slightly nudge them toward win–win trades and away from extreme, exploitative terms, but their primary objective remains maximizing their own payout within three short rounds.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone has 10 of each color and only their own values. Turtle knows blue is her best color and that red is still valuable; green is a modest, safe asset. Gorilla knows red is her best color and that blue is decent but not as strong as red; green is again a safe baseline. Zebra knows that none of red or blue is particularly good for her, and green dominates blue while red is only slightly better than blue. With full uncertainty about others’ exact valuations (they only know ranges), we expect small, low-risk proposals that test the waters and aim for obvious comparative advantages: turtle will likely try to trade away some red to acquire blue or occasionally green, gorilla will try small red-for-blue or red-for-green style trades, and zebra will be open to giving up blue or red in exchange for green when she can see even small, positive gains. Because it is the first round, they are unlikely to offer extreme or very unbalanced trades; they want their proposals to be accepted to learn about counterparts’ preferences while still earning a visible surplus.

After round 0, some structure has emerged. Turtle has revealed a willingness to pay red for blue, signaling that she values blue highly and red is not cheap but tradable. Gorilla has shown strong demand for red and willingness to part with blue at a premium ratio. Zebra has behaved as a classic low-value holder for blue and red, happily giving them up in exchange for modest gains and for green, confirming others’ suspicions that zebra’s valuations of red and blue are relatively low. Now, with slightly more informative history, proposals can become more targeted. Turtle will likely try again to buy blue from zebra or maybe from gorilla if the price is favorable. Gorilla, having accumulated some red, might be more cautious about overpaying in blue for additional red, but will still pursue red if it is cheap. Zebra, having earned comfortable positive surplus already and holding more green, will remain eager to convert remaining low-value blue (and possibly some red) into green or occasionally into combinations that slightly improve her bundle. Proposals will still be modest in size to avoid being rejected, but they will be more precise responses to inferred counterpart values.

Going into the final round, players have a clearer sense of each other’s priorities. Turtle is perceived as a blue-seeker who will trade red or green when the price is right but is no longer desperate after already improving her blue holdings. Gorilla is understood as a red-maximizer willing to give up blue and even some green when red is available at a good rate. Zebra has reinforced her role as a seller of red and blue in exchange for green or safer, slightly more valuable mixes. With only three final proposals left (one per player), each participant knows this is their last chance to adjust portfolios. Risk-averse zebra is now especially willing to accept any clearly positive, safe trade rather than wait for nonexistent future rounds. Gorilla may push slightly harder on terms that favor her red accumulation, recognizing that zebra’s outside options are now limited. Turtle, having observed that her round-1 green-for-blue offer was too aggressive and got no takers, will likely propose something more modest and safely acceptable if she trades at all. The final moves are therefore small, targeted trades that lock in last bits of surplus without risking rejection from overly lopsided terms.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features privately valued red and blue chips and common-value green chips. Everyone starts with 10 of each color. Green is worth $0.50 to all players, so trades of green only make sense as a way to indirectly buy more valuable colors. The key asymmetries are: (1) zebra-pink-2457 values blue very highly ($1.00) but red low ($0.40); (2) cat-green-4185 values red highest ($0.90) and blue moderately ($0.60); (3) iguana-yellow-3262 values red and blue both positively but with a smaller spread (red $0.80, blue $0.30). These differences create gains from trade: cat and iguana tend to want more red, zebra wants more blue, and all will be willing to part with colors relatively less valuable to them, especially green if needed. However, during the game each player only sees their own values and must infer others’ values from observed offers and acceptances. The surplus calculator ensures that no one will knowingly accept a proposal that is negative according to their own private values, so accepted trades will always be individually profitable. Strategic tension comes from: (a) uncertainty about others’ values—players must discover that zebra loves blue and that cat and iguana value red more than blue, (b) the fact that multiple acceptances lead to a random partner, so a player cannot ‘target’ the most profitable counterparty, and (c) a short horizon of three rounds, which encourages players to look for early, clearly positive trades rather than over-waiting for perfect deals. Given the personas, cat and iguana are numerically strong and cautious about losses; zebra may be more approximate but willing to explore. Overall, we expect relatively conservative first-round offers, then modest learning, and a few clearly positive trades focusing on moving red toward cat/iguana and blue toward zebra, with green occasionally used to sweeten terms.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, no trades have occurred and no one has information about others’ chip values beyond the common range. Players see symmetric initial holdings and know green is common-value at $0.50. Proposers will likely test the market with relatively small, low-risk trades that look attractive to them and that they hope might also appeal to others. Because each responder has a surplus calculator, only clearly positive deals will be accepted. Early accepted trades will reveal which colors particular players appear to value or undervalue, which in turn shapes subsequent offers in later rounds.

After round 0, there have been two accepted trades. First, cat traded 2 green for 2 blue with zebra; second, zebra traded 4 red for 3 blue with cat. Publicly, all players now see that cat and zebra are willing to trade green for blue and red for blue in specific ratios, hinting that both value blue meaningfully and that zebra is quite willing to give up red. Cat’s acceptance of 4 red for 3 blue reveals a strong relative taste for red. Iguana, who failed to trade in round 0, has slightly more information about others’ revealed preferences and will try to position offers accordingly. Holdings are now skewed: cat has more blue and red but fewer green; zebra has fewer red but still significant blue and green; iguana remains at baseline holdings. Proposers will try to exploit these revealed preferences, but they must still offer terms that are positive for themselves and for at least one responder under that responder’s unknown values.

Going into the final round, everyone has seen several accepted trades that highlight relative valuations: cat consistently trades away green and blue to acquire more red; zebra trades away red and green in order to consolidate blue; iguana has mainly used green as a bid for red so far, revealing a moderate preference for red but less aggressiveness. The horizon is about to end, so there is no reason to save chips for future bargaining. Proposers may be slightly more willing to extend generous but still positive offers to lock in final gains. Because of the random-partner rule when multiple players accept, proposers will still design offers likely to attract at least one clear counterparty, often targeting whoever currently seems to hold a surplus of the color they want. We expect final trades to further move red toward cat and iguana, and blue toward zebra, as long as each side’s calculator shows a gain.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player, three-round bargaining game features symmetric initial endowments (10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN each) but asymmetric private values on RED/BLUE. GREEN is common-value ($0.50 for everyone), so it acts as a natural low-risk medium for trade: giving up GREEN is always a real cost, but all players agree how big that cost is. The main gains from trade come from reallocating RED and BLUE from players who value them less to players who value them more. Bear and Peacock are symmetric high-value types (RED=$0.80, BLUE=$0.80 and $0.70 respectively), while Swan values BLUE highly ($0.80) but RED only moderately ($0.50). Efficient patterns would move RED from Swan toward Bear/Peacock and potentially BLUE from Peacock toward Bear/Swan if the rate is favorable. However, uncertainty is high: each participant only knows their own valuations and sees only ranges for others. They must infer whether others value RED/BLUE more or less, and whether GREEN is seen as a hard cash proxy or just another chip. Because response calculators show exact personal net gains/losses for any posted proposal, nobody has to guess whether a particular trade helps them—they only face uncertainty about future opportunities and about whether others will accept profitable offers. Strategic tension centers on: (1) not giving away too much surplus if others might have similar or lower values, (2) using GREEN as a safe sweetener versus hoarding it as guaranteed value, and (3) the random partner rule when multiple players accept, which makes targeting a specific partner risky and can induce more conservative or more symmetric offers. Personas suggest all three players are at least moderately cooperative and somewhat uncertainty-averse; Swan is numerically strong and reflective, likely to push for clear positive surplus and avoid near-zero trades, while Bear and Peacock may be slightly more cautious about risk and inclined to simple, easy-to-understand ratios. Across three short rounds, we should expect mostly conservative, clearly positive-sum trades: simple “1-for-1 plus a small premium” offers on RED↔BLUE or RED/BLUE↔GREEN, relatively few rejected offers once players see obviously positive calculators, and some path-dependence as early accepted trades change later bargaining power and chip inventories.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, everyone holds 10 of each chip. Nobody knows others’ exact RED/BLUE values, only that they lie between $0.10 and $1.00. GREEN is known to be $0.50 for all, so it is a natural anchor for early proposals. As the first proposer, Bear is likely to test the market with a simple, low-stakes offer that clearly benefits her given her relatively high and symmetric RED/BLUE values. Peacock, with slightly weaker BLUE value but similar RED, may try to upgrade from BLUE to RED at a modest premium in GREEN. Swan, who values RED less, will be inclined to trade away RED for either BLUE or GREEN at favorable rates, but only if her calculator shows a clear gain. Early trades provide public information: who is eager to acquire which color, and what kinds of exchange rates seem acceptable.

Going into round 1, Bear has slightly more RED and BLUE but fewer GREEN after profitable trades; Peacock has shifted toward RED at the expense of BLUE; Swan has given up some BLUE and taken GREEN, then attempted (unsuccessfully) to dispose of under-valued RED. The history suggests to everyone that RED is attractive to Bear and Peacock, and BLUE is somewhat attractive to Bear and Swan. Players now have a bit more information about relative preferences, though they still do not know exact values. Bear is likely to refine her portfolio further by swapping RED for GREEN or BLUE only if she can lock in a clear gain. Peacock now holds more RED than initially and fewer BLUE, so he may be willing to part with some RED at a premium if he suspects others highly value it. Swan, realizing that RED is not in demand at the last offered rate, may either improve the terms (offering more RED per BLUE) or switch to trading GREEN if she wants to secure guaranteed value.

By round 2, everyone has seen several offers and responses. It is now clearer that Bear values both RED and BLUE relatively highly and is willing to accept favorable ratios, that Peacock is willing to exchange GREEN for BLUE and has already upgraded part of his portfolio into RED, and that Swan is systematically trying to shed RED in favor of BLUE and GREEN. With only three proposal turns left, players balance the desire to squeeze out a bit more surplus against the risk of posting offers that go unaccepted. This encourages relatively generous, straightforward proposals that yield small but certain gains instead of aggressive, finely tuned trades that might be declined. Bear may look for any last opportunity to trade away some RED to Swan or Peacock only at very good rates; Peacock may seek to rebalance toward BLUE if he feels too RED-heavy; Swan will likely continue to offload RED even at slightly worse terms, as long as her calculator shows a significant positive gain and the trade is likely to be accepted.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN chips, and green is worth $0.50 to everyone. The strategic tension is entirely about RED and BLUE, which have different private values. Gorilla values both RED and BLUE at $0.30 (symmetric and relatively low), Camel values both at $0.50 (medium and equal), and Monkey values BLUE very highly at $0.90 and RED low at $0.20. Neither Camel nor Gorilla have a strong reason to trade RED for BLUE with each other because both colors are equal for each of them; any swap between only those two is zero-sum in value and thus unlikely unless they miscalculate. By contrast, Monkey has a strong incentive to convert RED into BLUE: every RED they give up loses $0.20 while every BLUE they gain adds $0.90, so roughly any rate where Monkey gives up no more than about 4–5 RED per BLUE is beneficial, and many trades are extremely attractive. Gorilla and Camel have symmetric values in RED and BLUE, so for them a unit-for-unit red–blue trade is neutral in value; to gain, they need a slightly favorable rate (e.g., getting 2 of one color for offering 1 of the other), but then the counterparty typically loses and will decline. Green chips are common-value at $0.50, so trades involving green only reshuffle but do not expand total surplus: a player will only give up green if they are gaining more total value in specialized colors. Uncertainty comes from not knowing others’ values: players only see their own payoff changes in the calculator. Camel and Monkey are both relatively numerate and should pay close attention to net-value changes; Gorilla is somewhat more uncertainty-averse and might propose safer, near-neutral trades. Because multiple acceptances lead to a random partner, senders must design trades that are attractive to exactly one other person if they want to target a specific counterpart, which is difficult without value information. Given the short, three-round structure (nine total turns) and simultaneous accept/decline, we expect modest learning: early offers may be exploratory, middle-round offers focus more on obvious gains (e.g., Camel trying to sell blue to Monkey), and late-round offers become more aggressive as time runs out. The main predicted flow is Camel and Gorilla trying to extract value from Monkey’s likely strong preference for blue, with Monkey accepting any clearly positive blue-for-red or blue-for-green deals and declining trades that sacrifice too much blue or green. Pure red–blue swaps between Camel and Gorilla are likely sparse and mostly declined, while trades reallocating blue toward Monkey at prices that still benefit Camel or Gorilla are most likely to be accepted.

rounds.round_explanation: In the opening round, no one yet knows how others value the colored chips. Gorilla moves first and, being relatively uncertainty-averse, is likely to test the waters with a small, near-neutral-looking trade. Camel, with high numeracy, will watch carefully for any sign of Monkey’s or Gorilla’s preferences and may immediately try to monetize blue if a pattern appears. Monkey, who values BLUE very highly, will be cautious about giving up blue but open to exchanging RED for BLUE or for GREEN at favorable rates. This round is primarily exploratory: players float simple offers to see who bites and infer relative valuations from acceptances and rejections.

After round 0, some structure has emerged. Monkey has revealed a willingness to trade away RED for either GREEN or BLUE, but has not sold BLUE nor GREEN, hinting that those may be relatively more valuable to him. Camel has already executed two mutually beneficial trades with Monkey and now holds more RED and BLUE but fewer GREEN. Gorilla’s exploratory, value-neutral red–blue offer was refused by both, suggesting neither Camel nor Monkey sees any edge in a simple 1:1 swap. In this round, Gorilla may attempt a slightly more ambitious but still relatively safe cross-color trade involving green; Camel is now more confident that Monkey likes BLUE and may craft offers to pull more chips that are neutral to Camel but valuable to Monkey. Monkey continues to prioritize acquiring BLUE while being more selective about giving up GREEN.

By the final round, patterns are clearer. Monkey has consistently accepted trades that increase his BLUE holdings at the expense of RED or GREEN but has never offered BLUE to others, reinforcing the inference that BLUE is his most prized chip. Camel has repeatedly structured trades that are modestly favorable according to his calculator and that shift BLUE toward Monkey while taking on more RED and GREEN. Gorilla, more cautious and less numerically aggressive, has made only one successful trade so far and might now attempt a final, slightly favorable but not extreme offer, hoping that one of the others accepts for their own reasons. With only three turns left, all players face a deadline: there is no future reputational cost to being tough, so proposals may be more one-sided and many will be declined unless they still give positive surplus to a counterpart. The most likely final agreement, if any, again routes BLUE to Monkey in exchange for RED or GREEN, while Gorilla either sits on his portfolio or executes one last modest trade.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This 3-player, 3-round game features private values for red and blue chips and common value for green. Everyone starts with 10 of each chip, so initial differences are purely in per-chip values: Alligator (A) values RED most (0.70) then GREEN (0.50) then BLUE (0.30); Otter (O) values BLUE most (0.50), GREEN (0.50), and RED least (0.30); Tiger (T) prefers RED (0.60) over GREEN (0.50) and dislikes BLUE most (0.20). Gains from trade arise when players exchange chips they value less for chips they value more, but each knows only their own valuation schedule, not the others’. The on-screen surplus calculator makes it easy for each player to avoid trades that are personally negative, so accepted trades should be non-loss-making for both participants ex ante. However, strategic uncertainty remains over whether a proposal is attractive to one or both potential recipients and, if both accept, which partner is randomly matched. With simultaneous accept/decline, recipients essentially face a take-it-or-leave-it offer with a lottery over who gets the deal when both want it. A, as an older and somewhat numerate but only moderately reflective decision-maker, is likely to propose conservative, clearly self-profitable trades, especially RED-for-BLUE where A offloads low-value BLUE and gets higher-value RED. O, highly cooperative, numerate, and risk-averse, is likely to craft more balanced trades, often supplying blue (which she values) only when she can upgrade into red at modest loss or into green/blue bundles that are roughly even or slightly favorable. T, numerate and somewhat present-focused, values red but dislikes blue the most, so is also inclined to trade away BLUE for RED or GREEN. Given symmetric interest in RED and GREEN and mutual distaste for BLUE, the market tends to push BLUE outward from A and T toward O only when she’s compensated enough, while RED flows from O toward A and T. Over three short rounds, the players have limited time to learn others’ valuations, so they will mostly rely on simple heuristics: starting close to perceived fairness (1:1 or 2:1 type trades) but structured so that, given their own values and uncertainty aversion, each proposed trade provides a small sure gain rather than a large risky one. We forecast a path where a few modest surplus-improving trades occur (especially RED-for-BLUE with O as a frequent recipient), with several proposals declined because they are unattractive to at least one potential recipient under private information and because senders cannot direct which accepting player they will trade with.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, all players hold 10 of each chip and know only their own values. Proposers will likely start with simple, nearly symmetric trades that guarantee them a visible gain given their private calculator while being plausibly acceptable to at least one other. Alligator, going first with no history, will likely test a modest RED-for-BLUE or RED-for-GREEN offer that clearly benefits him. Otter, highly numerate and cooperative, will tend to propose something close to fair but slightly in her favor, likely moving RED (which she values least) in return for BLUE or GREEN. Tiger, last in the round, observes earlier acceptances/declines and may infer a bit about who likes RED or BLUE, then craft a small trade to offload some BLUE for RED or GREEN.

After round 0, trade history reveals that Otter was willing to exchange some BLUE for RED at a modest loss, and that Alligator was willing to accept BLUE in return for RED at what appears to be a slight gain for him, revealing his strong preference for RED over BLUE. Tiger’s sale of BLUE for RED to Alligator shows that Tiger also dislikes BLUE relative to RED, and Alligator is happy to be a RED buyer. The pattern suggests emerging roles: Alligator as a RED buyer and BLUE sink, Otter as a potential BLUE buyer and RED seller, and Tiger trying to shed BLUE for RED. In round 1, Alligator, now holding more RED and BLUE than initially, might experiment with a GREEN-involving offer or try to buy more RED from Otter or Tiger. Otter, aware that both men like RED, may propose trades where she sells RED for BLUE or GREEN but with a slightly better rate for herself than in round 0. Tiger, seeing demand for RED from Alligator, will likely repeat or slightly scale up a BLUE-for-RED proposal directed implicitly at Alligator, though Otter might also be an occasional partner if the rate suits her.

By the final round, patterns are clearer: Alligator has consistently accepted trades where he gives up BLUE for RED, indicating his valuation gap between RED and BLUE is strong and positive. Otter has shown willingness to trade RED for BLUE when the ratio is close to 1:1 or slightly in favor of shedding RED, consistent with her lower value for RED. Tiger repeatedly offloads BLUE for RED, confirming his dislike of BLUE. The remaining strategic tension is about marginal improvements: each player will look for one more modest surplus-improving trade without taking on large risk or complexity. Alligator may be cautious about giving up more RED unless heavily compensated, but he may still accept slightly favorable BLUE-for-RED trades from Tiger. Otter might attempt one last modest RED-for-BLUE offer attractive to either man. Tiger will likely continue the theme of trading BLUE away for RED, particularly to Alligator, who has been a reliable counterparty. Because the horizon ends after this round, there is no reputational reason to be generous; everyone focuses on clean, calculator-confirmed gains.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game centers on trading red and blue chips, whose values vary across players, while green chips are a shared numeraire at $0.50 for everyone. All start with 10 of each color, so each player’s baseline portfolio is symmetric but privately valued differently. Dolphin cares most about BLUE ($1.00) and least about RED ($0.20); Frog values RED highest ($1.00) and BLUE low ($0.30); Pig is roughly balanced and modest on both RED and BLUE ($0.40 each). This creates large potential gains from trade: trades that move red chips toward Frog and blue chips toward Dolphin tend to be efficient, while Pig will often be on either side of deals when the price is favorable enough. However, players only see their own valuations: they know green is common-value at $0.50, and they know everyone’s red/blue values are somewhere between $0.10–$1.00, but not the exact numbers. The key uncertainty is: who values which color most? Early in the game, players will likely experiment conservatively, using green as a safe payment unit and proposing favorable but not extreme terms (e.g., trading away the color they personally value least). Because accept/reject choices are made privately and simultaneously, senders can’t target a specific recipient and must propose deals that might plausibly benefit at least one of the other two players. Over three short rounds, most players will avoid any trade that visibly loses money for them, so accepted trades tend to be clearly positive in private surplus and somewhat moderate in size. High empathy and cooperation orientations for all three players mildly support fairer pricing and willingness to trade if there’s any visible mutual gain, but strong uncertainty aversion (especially for Dolphin and, to a lesser extent, Frog) works against very lopsided or speculative terms. The forecast below assumes they make numerically sensible, low-risk proposals that gradually reveal others’ likely preferences, leading to a few selectively accepted trades that move chips toward their highest-value holders without anyone taking a visible loss.

rounds.round_explanation: At the outset, everyone has 10 of each chip and no information about others’ specific values, beyond the shared $0.50 value of green. Each player is likely to think of green as cash-like and be especially cautious about giving up greens cheaply. Early proposals will probably be small and exploratory, trading away each proposer’s personally weakest color in exchange for either green or the color they privately care about most. Because any accepting partner is chosen at random when multiple accept, senders will craft terms that could plausibly be attractive to at least one other player but still visibly positive for themselves.

After round 0, only one trade has gone through: Frog gave up 2 RED to Pig for 2 GREEN. Publicly, everyone now knows Frog was willing to part with some red to gain green, and Pig was willing to do the opposite, albeit on relatively even-looking terms from an outside perspective. This suggests, but does not prove, that Frog may not have especially low value on red, since trading 2 red for 2 green at common value is only mildly favorable for Frog, and that Pig also saw it as acceptable. Dolphin has had no trades yet, so others still know nothing about her valuations. Given the short remaining horizon, proposers will now be slightly bolder but still avoid clear losses, using the observed acceptance patterns to refine guesses: Frog might suspect Pig has at least moderate red value; Pig learns Frog values green at least as highly as red; Dolphin infers both are cautious but open to modest surplus trades.

Going into the final round, a partial pattern has emerged: Frog has traded away red only when getting at least equivalent green, and traded for red by paying in blue; Pig has been the counterparty in both accepted trades and appears flexible, roughly balancing red, blue, and green as long as the terms are not too unfavorable; Dolphin has now executed one profitable trade to acquire blue for green at a discount from her perspective. Even though exact private values remain unknown, each player now has rough directional beliefs: Dolphin probably values blue more than green, Frog probably values red strongly relative to blue, and Pig is willing to adjust his portfolio if the price is close. With only three turns left, players will either make one more targeted attempt to improve their portfolios or sit out unattractive offers; risk-averse tendencies and the absence of future rounds limit aggressive experimentation, so the remaining proposals are small, conservative, and aimed at clear private gains.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features two privately valued chip colors (RED and BLUE) and one common-value color (GREEN at $0.50 for everyone). All players start with 10 of each color. Because GREEN has the same value to everyone, it serves as a natural "currency" that is safe to give away or receive at a known rate. The key private-value tension is over RED and BLUE: iguana values BLUE highly and RED weakly (BLUE $0.80, RED $0.20), owl values RED highly and BLUE weakly (RED $0.70, BLUE $0.30), and squid values BLUE moderately and RED almost not at all (BLUE $0.50, RED $0.10). This creates clear surplus opportunities: trades where iguana or squid give RED to owl in exchange for BLUE or GREEN, or where owl gives BLUE to iguana or squid for GREEN. However, each player only knows their own values and must infer others' valuations from observed proposals and acceptances. Players see calculators showing their own net gains or losses for each offered trade, so they will not knowingly accept negative-surplus deals, but they might accept small positive gains even if larger gains would be possible with different partners. Over three rounds (nine total proposal turns), there is limited time, so players have some incentive to accept modest but safe improvements rather than wait for perfect trades that may never materialize. Iguana is numerate and patient and likely to seek sizable positive gains from shifting into BLUE and GREEN away from RED, perhaps experimenting early and then adjusting. Owl is highly uncertainty-averse and knows RED is precious to them, so they will be cautious in giving up RED and may be reluctant to give BLUE away too cheaply until they see evidence that others really want BLUE. Squid is cooperative and exploratory, so she may propose or accept more trades that yield small but positive gains, using GREEN frequently as a medium. Across the game, the likely dynamic is gradual revelation: early proposals signal who values which colors, and later rounds exploit this information more aggressively, with trades that move RED toward owl and BLUE toward iguana and squid. Some profitable deals will still fail because proposal sizes or chip colors do not align with the counterparties' private valuations or risk preferences, and simultaneous responses plus random tie-breaking when multiple players accept adds additional uncertainty about exactly which partner each proposer will end up trading with.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone holds 10 of each chip and has no direct information about others' private values. The main strategic considerations are exploratory: players want to propose trades that are clearly profitable for themselves while being plausibly acceptable to at least one counterpart. Iguana is likely to test the waters using RED (which he values little) to try to buy BLUE or GREEN. Owl, who values RED highly, may experiment with trading BLUE for GREEN or RED, but is cautious. Squid, relatively cooperative and exploratory, may accept small sure gains and propose modest, symmetric-looking trades that feel fair. Early accepted trades will start to reveal who is eager to give up which colors, shaping expectations for later rounds.

After round 0, iguana has revealed a willingness to give up RED for BLUE and GREEN, suggesting to others that RED may be relatively cheap for him and BLUE more valuable. Squid has shown that she will accept modest but positive trades and is ready to use GREEN as a safe medium. Owl's declined offer (BLUE for GREEN) showed that her initial pricing was not attractive to others and that she may have overestimated how badly others want BLUE at that rate. Strategic considerations now shift toward exploiting these early signals: iguana can keep shedding low-value RED for value in BLUE or GREEN, owl can cautiously adjust BLUE-for-GREEN exchange rates to make them more appealing, and squid can continue to farm small, safe gains using her willingness to accept decent but not extreme deals.

Entering the final round, players have clearer signals: iguana repeatedly takes BLUE and GREEN and gives up RED, revealing strong BLUE preference and weak RED valuation. Owl has collected some extra RED and seems reluctant to give it up cheaply, while her attempts to offload BLUE at earlier rates were unappealing. Squid appears happy to take BLUE when fairly priced and to turn low-value RED into GREEN or BLUE. With only three proposal turns left, players may relax a bit on bargaining margins: they are more willing to accept small but safe gains rather than hold out for ideal trades they might not get. Iguana will likely finalize his portfolio by swapping away residual RED for GREEN or BLUE. Owl might either stop trading or make one last cautious attempt to turn BLUE into GREEN or RED at gentler prices. Squid may seize any final low-risk opportunities where the calculator shows a clearly positive gain, especially if they increase her BLUE holdings or GREEN without sacrificing too much.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN chips. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone. The key asymmetry is in RED and BLUE values. Gorilla-purple values BLUE very low ($0.10) and RED moderately ($0.40). Llama-pink and whale-orange both value BLUE highly ($0.90) and RED at $0.60. This creates clear gains-from-trade: gorilla wants to convert low-value BLUE into higher-value RED or GREEN; the others want to convert RED into BLUE, and view GREEN as neutral relative to their private chips but useful as a medium of exchange. However, players only know their own payoffs, so each must infer others’ values from observed proposals and acceptances across just three rounds. With simultaneous responses and random recipient when multiple accept, proposers face uncertainty about which partner they will trade with and must craft deals that are attractive to at least one counterparty. Personas suggest whale-orange is relatively analytical and cooperative, llama-pink is opportunistic and more risk-tolerant, and gorilla-purple is cooperative but more uncertainty-averse. Across the nine proposal turns, we would expect early exploratory offers with modest surplus for the proposer that test whether BLUE is generally valued by others and whether RED is costly to others, followed by more targeted RED-for-BLUE and BLUE-for-RED offers once some pattern emerges. Because GREEN is common-value and modestly priced, it often appears as a balancing chip in trades: slightly sweetening deals without revealing much about private valuations. Some mutually beneficial trades should occur, but frictions—limited time, incomplete information, risk of overpaying, and the random selection when both responders accept—will also lead to declined offers, especially if terms are too skewed toward the proposer or if uncertainty-averse players avoid marginal or ambiguous trades.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, no one has observed any trades, so each only knows their own values. Gorilla-purple knows BLUE is weak for her and will likely test trading away BLUE or acquiring more RED. Llama-pink and whale-orange each know they strongly prefer BLUE to RED and may probe whether others will part with BLUE cheaply. Since values for GREEN are common but unknown to players, it may be tentatively used in small amounts. Given uncertainty, initial offers are likely modest and somewhat conservative, intended as information-gathering: players see who accepts which side of RED–BLUE–GREEN combos and at what ratios. Risk- and uncertainty-tolerant players (especially llama-pink and whale-orange) are somewhat more willing to accept slightly risky but positive-expected-value deals, while gorilla-purple may avoid trades that look close to break-even to her.

After round 0, gorilla-purple and llama-pink have completed a profitable BLUE-for-RED trade that reveals partial information: gorilla is willing to give up multiple BLUE for fewer RED, implying BLUE is relatively cheap for her; llama-pink is willing to give up RED for BLUE, hinting BLUE may be more valuable to him. Whale-orange, having seen only that one trade and two declined offers (including his own attempt to buy BLUE), infers that others are reluctant to part with BLUE cheaply and that gorilla’s acceptance threshold for giving up BLUE is above a certain implicit price. Players now face a classic thin-market problem: they know there are gains from trade but must adjust terms enough to get acceptance in only a few remaining turns. We expect gorilla-purple to continue trying to move BLUE for RED but on slightly more favorable terms, llama-pink to refine offers towards gorilla or whale-orange that still yield him value from acquiring BLUE, and whale-orange either to pivot towards safer trades using GREEN or to improve his BLUE-acquisition terms to clear at least one deal.

By the final round, some patterns are clearer: gorilla-purple has shown a willingness to trade away BLUE for RED but only when the implicit price is favorable to her; llama-pink and whale-orange have directly traded with each other, confirming that both happily swap RED for BLUE at roughly even or slightly favorable ratios, suggesting to each that they share similar preferences. With just three proposal turns left, players weigh marginal improvements against the risk of offering terms that are too generous. Gorilla-purple may still try to unload residual low-value BLUE, but past rejections make her cautious. Llama-pink and whale-orange, now both quite BLUE-heavy, may pivot to using GREEN in small quantity to rebalance or to take low-risk, slightly beneficial trades if available. However, as their portfolios converge—both holding lots of high-value BLUE and similar RED levels—the pure gains-from-trade shrink. The likely outcome is one more modestly beneficial trade, if any, with other proposals failing because they either don’t improve the responder’s payoff or involve swapping nearly equivalent-value bundles where the net gain is too small relative to perceived risk or effort.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game is driven by asymmetric private values for red and blue chips, plus a common value for green. Every player starts with 10 of each color, but tiger-purple-9111 values red and especially blue far more than the others (BLUE=$1.00, RED=$0.80), while duck-purple-4961 and lion-yellow-4229 have relatively low and closer valuations. Green is common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so trades involving green do not exploit private-value differences; they mainly reshuffle risk or balance bundles. The main surplus opportunities come from tiger trading away low-value-to-others chips for high-value-to-him chips, or vice versa, subject to the fact that no one knows others’ values. Tiger knows only that there is some heterogeneity, not that he is the outlier who values red and blue so highly. Duck and lion likely suspect that some others might value colors differently but do not know where they stand. With three rounds and one turn per player per round, strategic timing matters: actors with early turns can probe the market with exploratory offers, while later turns in a round respond to new information revealed by who accepted previous offers. However, information is limited: each player only sees accept/decline behavior and aggregate holdings, not others’ monetary values. The calculator interface means that on each proposal and response, players know exactly their own net dollar gain/loss from that specific trade, which makes dominated trades (personal monetary losses) very unlikely to be accepted. Thus, viable offers must be weakly individually rational for at least one responder. Because multiple acceptances lead to a random recipient, proposers cannot choose their partner, so they will typically design offers that would be at least acceptable to either potential counterparty, or else they will try to narrowly appeal to one player while risking that both decline. Given the personas, all three are relatively uncertainty-averse and at least moderately cooperative, but they are still payoff-maximizing: modest positive gains will be accepted, and players will hesitate to engineer large, risky, late-game trades that could backfire. Over the three rounds, we should expect a pattern of (1) cautious, near break-even or slightly favorable offers in round 0 as players test the waters; (2) modest adjustment in round 1 as they infer who might value which colors more from revealed acceptances; and (3) possibly more assertive attempts by tiger to accumulate blue and red by round 2 once it becomes clearer that others are willing to part with them cheaply from his perspective, while duck and lion may opportunistically trade if they see small but safe personal gains. Still, because everyone rejects negative expected-value trades and no one sees others’ values, we expect only a few accepted deals where both sides see a non-trivial personal surplus, with several tentative, exploratory offers being declined when they look unprofitable to the responders.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 of each chip color and have no evidence about others’ private values. They know that values can differ, but not the pattern. Given risk and uncertainty aversion, early offers will tend to be simple and small in size, often involving one high-value color for the proposer in exchange for another color the proposer suspects might be more valuable to others. Round 0 thus functions as a probing stage: duck goes first and is likely to make a cautious, slightly favorable trade from her own perspective, hoping to discover whether either lion or tiger is willing to give up something she values. Lion then reacts with his own exploratory proposal, shaped by whether he sees anyone accept duck’s offer. Finally, tiger, who privately has the strongest preferences for red and blue, will likely make an assertive but not overly generous offer to acquire red or blue, testing how cheaply he can get them without revealing his strong preferences too clearly.

Entering round 1, holdings have shifted only via lion’s accepted trade with tiger: lion now has slightly more red and fewer green chips, while tiger has fewer red and more green. Duck’s and tiger’s initial offers were declined, signaling that equal-rate swaps involving green for red or blue at 1:1 are not very attractive to responders given common green value and their own valuations. Lion has learned that tiger will accept symmetric trades that are at least break-even for him in green-red terms, which for tiger were slightly positive thanks to his high red value. Duck has learned that neither lion nor tiger found trading red for green at par attractive. In this round, duck is likely to refine her ask, offering red or blue that she values at $0.50 and $0.20, respectively, to see if someone is willing to part with another color at a slightly favorable rate. Lion, now holding extra red, may try to swap some red for blue or even trade back for green if he believes others value it differently. Tiger, realizing he can profitably trade away common-value green in exchange for red or especially blue, may push a bit harder to obtain blue at a rate that still looks at least weakly acceptable to his counterpart.

Before round 2, several informative trades have occurred. Lion traded green for red with tiger in round 0, and then received green for blue from duck in round 1. Duck traded green for blue with lion and later traded blue to tiger for green, revealing to both counterparts that she is willing to move green at a slight personal loss if it leads to larger perceived gains, and that she does not highly prize blue relative to green.
