With a bit more structure in their beliefs, proposals may become bolder, featuring slightly larger chip quantities and more targeted directions (red toward Zebra, blue toward Turtle), but players still avoid explicit losses thanks to the calculator.

Entering the final round, the trade history indicates a strong pattern: Turtle has repeatedly paid to acquire blue and is willing to dump red; Gorilla has systematically unloaded red and at least once was willing to trade blue away if the terms were favorable; Zebra has shown willingness to part with blue to obtain red. This collectively reveals the approximate ranking: Zebra highly values red, Turtle highly values blue, and Gorilla places the lowest value on red among the three. With only three proposal turns left, each player has incentive to make one more targeted trade to sharpen their portfolio. Zebra now aims to convert remaining blue into red or, second-best, green, but must ensure any final trade is positive on her calculator, and she may anticipate that Turtle will still pay for blue. Gorilla, having improved her position already, might be more cautious, avoiding trades that risk giving up her relatively better-valued blue for merely neutral green unless the ratio is attractive. Turtle, with strong blue holdings and plenty of low-value red, still has motive to trade red away if any partner is willing, but she may now face stiffer resistance because Zebra and Gorilla have less red to spare and clearer preferences. Strategic uncertainty remains due to random partner selection if multiple players accept, so offers that are too generous to both other players risk pairing with a less-desired partner; in practice, each proposal is likely structured to be clearly attractive to exactly one counterpart.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game is driven by differences in how much each player privately values red and blue chips, while green chips have the same value ($0.50) for everyone. All start with 10 of each color. Cat values BLUE=$0.70, RED=$0.50; Iguana values RED=$0.80, BLUE=$0.30; Zebra values BLUE=$0.80, RED=$0.40. So Iguana is the red-specialist, Zebra is the blue-specialist, and Cat is moderately blue-oriented but also sees red and green as equal mid-value. Because each player only knows their own values, they have to infer others’ valuations from observed behavior, but the in-round calculators tell them the exact gain or loss for any proposed or received offer, so they never accept negative-surplus trades. Efficient trades shift red chips toward Iguana and blue chips toward Zebra (and somewhat toward Cat) while using green chips as a neutral medium of exchange. However, bargaining is complicated by (1) uncertainty about others’ exact valuations and reservation values, (2) three-player competition where an attractive offer may draw multiple acceptances and be randomly assigned, and (3) a short horizon of only three rounds, which makes players more willing to accept modest positive gains rather than waiting for a perfect deal. The personas suggest all three are relatively cooperative and numerate, so offers are likely to be mildly generous and clearly positive-sum for both sides, with incremental improvements in holdings rather than extreme or exploitative trades. Over the three rounds, we should see Iguana working to acquire underpriced red and shed blue, Zebra working to acquire blue and shed red, and Cat flexibly arbitraging between them with both red and blue for green and/or the opposite color, as long as Cat’s calculator stays positive. By the end, red will be more concentrated with Iguana, blue more with Zebra and Cat, and green somewhat redistributed as a balancing currency, with each accepted trade yielding small but clearly positive surplus for both parties involved.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, all players sit at 10 of each chip. Nobody knows others’ exact valuations, but each will quickly learn that some offers are accepted only when they are clearly positive-sum. Cat moves first and is likely to experiment with a modest, safe trade that yields a small positive surplus and tests how others value colors. Iguana, strongly preferring red and disliking blue, will look for ways to turn blue into red or green. Zebra, strongly preferring blue and mildly disliking red, will be inclined to trade away red for blue or possibly for green, but will be cautious not to give up too much. Because the second mover in a round can condition on prior observed trades, Iguana and Zebra will already start updating beliefs about relative values after Cat’s first offer. However, all three will prioritize modest but guaranteed gains over aggressive, risky offers this early.

Entering round 1, players have already seen that Cat and Zebra are willing to trade blue against red and that Iguana is happy to move blue away in exchange for green. Cat now holds slightly more blue and green and fewer red chips, Iguana holds more green and fewer blue, and Zebra is unchanged from the declined third turn. Beliefs begin to form: Zebra can infer that both Cat and Iguana are willing to take blue only when appropriately compensated; Iguana infers that Cat is relatively comfortable surrendering green for blue. With only two rounds left after this one, players will try to lock in more advantageous positions but still avoid any risky or low-margin trades. Cat’s next move will likely leverage his extra green to gain more blue, Iguana will keep turning blue into higher-valued red, and Zebra, having had a declined offer, becomes slightly more conservative and looks for clearer win–win structures, likely using her red to get more blue or green if someone values red more.

By the final round, trading patterns are clear: Iguana consistently pays with low-value blue to get either red or green, Zebra trades away red to improve her blue and green mix, and Cat positions himself as a flexible middleman, using green and his moderate valuations to capture small gains on both sides. Everyone now has informative signals about others’ relative tastes, even if not exact dollar values. With no future rounds, this is the last chance to improve payoffs, so players will accept any clearly positive trade that fits their portfolio goals, and proposers may lean slightly more generous to ensure acceptance. Cat will fine-tune his holdings, likely happy to exchange a bit of green or red to pick up more blue if profitable. Iguana tries once more to convert remaining blue into red or green, and Zebra aims to offload some residual red for higher-valued blue or green. Because there is no continuation value, each player’s acceptance rule becomes very simple: accept if and only if the calculator shows a strictly positive gain.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with symmetric chip counts (10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN) but asymmetric private values for RED and BLUE. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 for everyone. The key efficiency gains come from moving RED chips toward bear (who values RED at $0.70, higher than the others) and BLUE chips toward bear and swan (who value BLUE at $1.00 and $0.90, respectively) away from peacock (who values BLUE at only $0.40). Because GREEN is common-value, it is neutral in aggregate but can be used as a low-conflict medium of exchange: everyone values it the same, and it sits between players’ red/blue valuations. However, the UI makes each player very aware of their own gain/loss on every proposed trade, so they are unlikely to accept any strictly negative-surplus deals and will tend to counter only if they see at least a small positive gain. The main strategic uncertainty is about others’ private values: for example, bear knows she likes BLUE and RED more than GREEN, but does not know peacock’s or swan’s valuations; similarly, peacock may suspect that others value BLUE more (because of the public range and his own relatively low value) but cannot be sure. This fuels cautious opening offers, small and nearly fair trades, and a tendency to experiment in early turns to see what others might accept. Because acceptance decisions are simultaneous and the recipient is randomly chosen if multiple players accept, proposers face a coordination risk: they must design offers that are attractive to at least one target but not so attractive that they are indifferent about who gets it. Given the personas, bear and peacock are relatively cooperation-oriented and somewhat uncertainty-averse, leading them to prefer clean, low-risk trades that are clearly positive for both sides, while swan is more calculative and numerate and may be more aggressive in sniping favorable offers. Overall, we expect relatively modest, surplus-positive trades that mostly move BLUE from peacock to swan and bear, with some RED and GREEN used to compensate, and a few declined proposals when counterparties see only small or negative gains.

rounds.round_explanation: At the outset, everyone has symmetric 10–10–10 holdings and no direct information about others’ values. Round 0 is likely to feature cautious, exploratory offers. Bear, who highly values BLUE and somewhat values RED, will probably try a small, clearly positive-sum trade that uses GREEN or RED to buy BLUE. Peacock, who has relatively low value for BLUE and middling for RED, is the natural BLUE seller but does not yet know that others value BLUE much more; he may still test offering BLUE in exchange for GREEN or RED. Swan, who strongly values BLUE and is indifferent or low on RED, will be alert for profitable chances to pick up BLUE cheaply and may attempt slightly more assertive trades based on her strong numeracy. Trades that give a clear positive gain to at least one counterparty are likely to be accepted; ambiguous or nearly zero-gain trades will be declined.

After Round 0, BLUE has already started flowing from peacock to both bear and swan, compensated with GREEN. Peacock learns that others are quite willing to trade GREEN for his BLUE, suggesting that they probably value BLUE more than he does. Bear and swan both confirm that they can profitably buy BLUE with GREEN at favorable terms. This makes subsequent bargaining more strategic: peacock becomes more cautious about selling too much BLUE too cheaply, and bear and swan recognize they may be in competition for peacock’s remaining BLUE. Bear will likely try to acquire more BLUE while perhaps beginning to offload some RED, particularly if she suspects someone values RED reasonably well. Peacock might experiment with reversing some earlier terms or trying to sell BLUE for RED instead of GREEN to improve his own surplus. Swan, realizing that her earlier trade was profitable, may look for slightly more aggressive BLUE-for-RED or BLUE-for-GREEN ratios.

By Round 2, the players have seen several profitable BLUE-for-GREEN and BLUE-for-RED trades. Peacock has sold a substantial portion of his BLUE and has picked up some combination of GREEN and RED, improving his overall surplus relative to his low BLUE valuation. Bear holds more BLUE and still values it highly, but her willingness to give up additional RED or GREEN now reflects diminishing marginal gains: further BLUE may be worth less at the margin. Swan has also acquired additional BLUE and possibly some RED, and her numeracy makes her particularly cautious about late trades that might only generate tiny gains. With only three proposal turns left, players know that the opportunity to adjust portfolios is limited. Bear may attempt a final small, high-margin trade for BLUE or to swap unwanted chips; peacock may try to offload any remaining less-valued BLUE for better-valued chips; and swan will be selective, accepting only clearly positive-surplus offers and perhaps making one last targeted bid to fine-tune her holdings.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
All three players start with symmetric chip holdings (10 of each color) but different private values for red and blue chips. Green chips are common-value at $0.50 for everyone, so they are neutral in trade and serve mostly as a numeraire/transfer tool.

Private values:
- gorilla-yellow-5964 (Gorilla): RED=$1.00, BLUE=$0.10, GREEN=$0.50
- camel-red-6481 (Camel): RED=$0.30, BLUE=$0.60, GREEN=$0.50
- monkey-yellow-524 (Monkey): RED=$1.00, BLUE=$0.90, GREEN=$0.50

Gorilla values red highly and blue very little; Camel has the opposite ranking (blue relatively high, red low); Monkey values both red and blue highly, with a slight preference for blue over red. This creates clear gains from trade: Gorilla and Camel can profitably swap red for blue, and Camel may also want red from Monkey if Monkey demands enough blue in return. Monkey, with both red and blue valuable, is in a strong position and will be reluctant to give up either color without substantial compensation.

Uncertainty comes from each player not knowing others’ exact valuations. Gorilla knows only that other players might value red and blue differently, based on the public range, and infers preferences from which proposals get accepted. The simultaneous, private accept/decline and random tie-breaking among multiple accepters create strategic uncertainty: a proposer cannot target a specific partner once multiple players are willing to accept, so strategically generous proposals risk being claimed by whichever responder finds them best.

Given the UI calculator, each player clearly sees their own net gain or loss on any proposed trade. Rationally, we expect them never to accept a negative-surplus trade. However, bounded rationality, limited time, and social preferences may lead to slightly non-maximizing choices or conservative rejections of small gains. Over three rounds (nine proposal turns), players can adjust based on observed behavior: early rounds help reveal who values which chips; later rounds may feature more targeted, mutually beneficial trades as players infer each other’s types.

We should expect: (1) Gorilla offering cheap blue to acquire red, especially from Camel; (2) Camel trying to turn red into blue while occasionally using green as a sweetener; and (3) Monkey holding out for favorable deals given high values for both red and blue, accepting only clearly positive-surplus trades. Some good deals may be left on the table because of misaligned expectations, fear of accidentally giving too much to an unseen high-valuer, or simple miscoordination.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, no one has yet observed others’ behavior, so beliefs about counterparts’ values are diffuse. Everyone knows green is neutral for all, but the exact red/blue values of others are unknown. We expect relatively cautious, exploratory proposals that are clearly favorable from the proposer’s perspective but may be too one-sided for receivers. Gorilla is likely to try to move blue away and gain red; Camel is likely to test interest in exchanging red for blue; Monkey, valuing both red and blue highly, will be selective. Early rejections will inform later proposals by indicating which directions of trade are attractive or unattractive to each counterpart.

After Round 0, everyone has learned something. Gorilla successfully traded blue for red with Camel, revealing to both that Camel is willing to give up some red when sufficiently compensated and that Gorilla is eager to acquire red. Monkey’s rejection of Camel’s first offer and of Monkey’s own blue-for-green idea suggests Monkey values both non-green colors highly and is cautious about losing either at a discount. In Round 1, Gorilla should continue converting low-value blue into high-value red, likely targeting Camel again. Camel may adjust, offering less red or demanding more in return, and may start using green as a sweetener. Monkey, knowing others are keen on red/blue transfers, may only accept trades that clearly improve Monkey’s own already-strong portfolio.

By Round 2, several patterns are clearer. Gorilla has repeatedly given up low-value blue for high-value red and is now relatively red-rich and blue-poor. Camel has been the main counterparty, gradually exporting red to Gorilla and experimenting with green as a payment instrument, and has just sold a red chip to Monkey for green—indicating Camel is comfortable parting with red when compensated even by neutral-value chips. Monkey, after cautious early behavior, has finally accepted a modest but positive-surplus trade to gain another valuable red chip. In this final round, Gorilla may attempt one last blue-for-red conversion from Camel if remaining blue is available; Camel might try to rebalance back toward blue or green while not giving away too much surplus; Monkey, already strong in high-value colors, will only accept clearly positive deals and may propose a targeted trade that leverages Monkey’s slightly higher valuation for blue over green.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game features common-value GREEN chips ($0.50 for everyone) and privately valued RED and BLUE chips. Each player starts with 10 of each color. Values: Alligator: BLUE $0.60, RED $0.10; Otter: BLUE $1.00, RED $0.40; Tiger: BLUE $0.90, RED $0.70. GREEN is mid-value for everyone, so trades involving GREEN are mostly ways to arbitrage differing RED/BLUE values while using GREEN as a neutral medium. Otter and Tiger both value BLUE and RED relatively highly, with Otter especially loving BLUE and Tiger especially loving RED. Alligator values both colored chips less and GREEN equally, so he is naturally positioned as a potential supplier of low-value chips (especially RED) in exchange for GREEN or BLUE. Because proposals are evaluated with an on-screen surplus calculator, each accept/decline decision is effectively a myopic value comparison: players rarely accept negative-surplus trades. However, uncertainty remains because each player does not know others’ exact valuations and must infer them from observed trades. Strategic tension arises from: (1) the random recipient rule when multiple accept, which pushes proposers to construct offers that are acceptable to exactly one target or at least not attractive to unintended partners; (2) the short horizon (3 rounds × 3 turns) which limits opportunities to “signal” and instead encourages taking positive-surplus trades when available; and (3) the fact that Otter is likely relatively cooperative but still value-aware, Tiger somewhat opportunistic under risk, and Alligator older and careful but numerate. Likely dynamics: early in Round 0, offers are conservative and exploratory, often using GREEN against RED to search for someone willing to trade. Once Tiger or Otter identifies that the others value RED less than they do, they will try to accumulate RED cheaply, occasionally sacrificing a bit of BLUE or GREEN. Otter, with the highest BLUE valuation, is reluctant to give up BLUE unless the trade is clearly favorable in dollar terms. By Rounds 1 and 2, trade history shapes beliefs: if someone repeatedly refuses to sell BLUE, others infer high BLUE value, and offers pivot towards RED-for-GREEN or BLUE-for-RED. Overall, we expect few trades that reduce anyone’s private value; when trades do happen, they typically involve someone upgrading low-value RED into GREEN or BLUE, and Tiger/Otter converting GREEN into higher-valued RED/BLUE. Because partner choice is random when multiple accept, proposers sometimes slightly overtune offers (e.g., making them acceptable only to the player their behavior hints has lower or higher valuation of a specific color). Over three rounds, a small number of mutually beneficial trades are likely, but players remain cautious about giving away their best-valued color, especially BLUE for Otter and RED for Tiger.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, everyone has symmetric holdings (10 of each color) and no direct information about others’ valuations beyond the public chip ranges. Proposals in this round are likely exploratory, probing how willing others are to exchange RED and BLUE against GREEN. Alligator, who values RED very little, may try to offload RED for GREEN or BLUE. Otter and Tiger will be very reluctant to part with BLUE cheaply and will tend to accept trades where they upgrade GREEN or low-value chips into higher-valued RED/BLUE. Because there is no trade history yet, rejections are common as players feel out what kinds of offers draw interest. Any accepted trade will strongly shape subsequent beliefs about which color each counterpart is willing to sell.

Going into Round 1, Alligator has signaled a willingness to sell RED cheaply and buy GREEN, and Otter has revealed that she is happy to convert GREEN into RED at favorable terms. Both Otter and Tiger just rejected attempts to buy their BLUE and RED, respectively, at relatively neutral rates, suggesting they value those colors more than the proposer anticipated. This round, proposers adjust: Alligator may refine RED-for-GREEN offers, Otter may now try to acquire RED or BLUE more aggressively but will be wary of overpaying with GREEN, and Tiger may start specifically targeting Alligator as a cheap source of RED rather than Otter. The players also become more cautious about offers that benefit an unintended partner due to the random acceptance rule, so offers may be slightly more selective (e.g., giving more surplus to one likely partner than to the other).

By Round 2, trade history has clarified some patterns: Alligator is comfortable sacrificing RED and even some BLUE for GREEN; Otter is willing to give up a small amount of BLUE if she can gain enough RED; and Tiger actively accumulates RED and is ready to part with GREEN to get either RED or BLUE. Holdings are now asymmetric: Alligator has extra GREEN and a bit more BLUE but fewer RED; Otter has gained RED at the cost of some GREEN and BLUE; Tiger has accumulated RED but spent GREEN and one BLUE. With only one proposal each remaining, everyone seeks clear positive-surplus trades that fit these revealed preferences. Alligator may try one last time to offload low-value RED for GREEN or BLUE, Otter may attempt a subtle arbitrage if she sees surplus, and Tiger may seek a final RED or BLUE upgrade if the terms are right. However, because many of the easiest Pareto-improving trades have already been used, more proposals are now declined as margins tighten and players avoid giving away their highest-valued colors.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This is a three-player, three-round bargaining game where each player starts with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN chips. GREEN chips are common-value ($0.50 for everyone), but RED and BLUE chips are privately valued and asymmetric: dolphin values both RED and BLUE very little ($0.10 each), frog values RED very highly ($0.90) and BLUE at $0.50, and pig has moderate values for both ($0.40 RED, $0.30 BLUE). Because everyone knows GREEN is valuable to all and can infer that other colors might be heterogeneous, key incentives revolve around moving low-value chips to someone who values them more, in exchange for GREEN or for chips the proposer values more. Proposers see their own surplus from any drafted proposal, and responders see their surplus from accepting; this makes all trades effectively voluntary surplus-improving deals from each side’s own perspective. Uncertainty centers on what the others’ private valuations are and whether a given offer is good enough for at least one counterpart to accept in a setting where multiple acceptances lead to a random partner. With only three rounds, each player has limited opportunities to propose, which encourages them to pursue clearly positive-value trades rather than wait for perfect information. Given the value structure: (1) frog strongly wants RED and is relatively rich in value terms compared with others, (2) dolphin is effectively trying to turn cheap RED/BLUE into GREEN, and (3) pig is intermediate and can gain by arbitraging between frog’s strong demand for RED and dolphin’s willingness to part with RED/BLUE. Likely dynamics: early rounds feature exploratory but value-positive offers involving GREEN for RED/BLUE, with increasing willingness to accept mildly beneficial trades as time runs out; competition risk (multiple acceptances) pushes proposers to design offers that are especially attractive to one targeted counterparty but still at least non-terrible for the other.

rounds.round_explanation: In round 0, players are just starting to feel out each other’s likely preferences. Everyone knows GREEN is valuable to all, but the private values of RED and BLUE are unknown. Dolphin, with low values on both RED and BLUE, is naturally inclined to sell those for GREEN; frog, who values RED highly, seeks to accumulate RED, ideally paying with BLUE or possibly GREEN; pig is in the middle and can profit by swapping whichever color is worth less to them for something worth more. First proposals will likely be modest and conservative, testing acceptance thresholds without risking losses, and acceptance thresholds will be relatively high because future rounds are still available.

By round 1, the history has revealed some structure: dolphin is willing to trade away RED and BLUE for GREEN, pig is willing to buy GREEN with BLUE and to buy RED with GREEN, and frog is clearly interested in RED but couldn’t close a deal at 1-for-1 with BLUE. This gives everyone partial information: pig sees profitable arbitrage opportunities between dolphin and frog, while dolphin sees that pig, rather than frog, is their best GREEN source so far. Frog learns they may need to concede more value (likely with GREEN) to attract RED. With two rounds left (including this one), players remain selective but are more willing to accept small gains, and proposers target more specific counterparties based on inferred preferences.

Round 2 is the final chance to trade. At this point, players have a clearer sense of who values which colors: frog’s persistent attempts to buy RED, dolphin’s repeated willingness to trade RED and BLUE away for GREEN, and pig’s readiness to arbitrage between them. With no future rounds, each player’s acceptance threshold drops: any non-negative surplus trade may now be acceptable. Proposers focus on simple, obviously beneficial offers to at least one counterpart, but some promising trades may still be declined if they threaten to give away too much to a competitor or create low payoffs for the proposer after earlier deals. Final offers tend to be more aggressive in the direction of each player’s core interests (dolphin for GREEN, frog for RED, pig for intermediate improvements).

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with symmetric chip quantities (10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN), but they value RED and BLUE chips differently while GREEN is common-value ($0.50) for everyone. Owl-green-168 values RED and BLUE the most overall (RED $1.00, BLUE $0.60), squid-purple-9957 is close behind (RED $0.90, BLUE $0.50), and iguana-blue-1059 has the lowest valuations (RED $0.30, BLUE $0.20). This creates strong gains-from-trade: iguana has cheap chips that are relatively valuable to the others, especially RED and BLUE, while GREEN is neutral for everyone. Because each player only sees their own values, they know there are likely differences but not the exact ranking; however, they can learn about others’ likely preferences from which offers are proposed and accepted. In efficient trades, RED and BLUE should flow from iguana (low values) toward owl and squid (high values), while GREEN might move in the opposite direction as a balancing currency. The proposer in each turn can request one color and offer one different color, and all responders see whether their own payoff would go up or down from that trade; they are unlikely to accept any proposal that visibly lowers their own dollar payoff. Random selection when multiple players accept makes it risky for a proposer to design offers that are valuable to the highest-value counterpart only; if both others accept, the proposer cannot choose which partner they get. Over three short rounds, players face uncertainty about others’ valuations, limited time to experiment, and pressure to propose clearly beneficial trades that at least one counterpart will accept, without becoming so generous that they leave value on the table.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of Round 0, all players hold 10 of each chip. Everyone knows GREEN is $0.50 each, but only iguana sees his low RED/BLUE values, only owl sees her high RED (and decent BLUE) value, and only squid sees her high RED/BLUE values. Early offers will likely be exploratory and somewhat conservative, as each player feels out whether others strongly value RED or BLUE. Iguana has the biggest incentive to sell off RED/BLUE for GREEN (which is relatively more valuable to him), while owl and squid have incentive to acquire RED (and secondarily BLUE) even at the cost of GREEN. Rejections in this round mostly reflect that the calculator shows a loss or too small a gain for responders.

Going into Round 1, a major trade has already shifted holdings: iguana traded away RED for GREEN with owl, revealing that at least one counterpart is willing to pay GREEN for RED. Owl now has more RED (which she values highly) and fewer GREEN, while iguana is more GREEN-heavy and RED-poor. Squid has learned that someone else likes RED, and owl has some evidence that iguana is willing to part with RED at modest prices. Proposers will now start to refine offers: iguana may continue to trade away BLUE for GREEN, owl may try to swap out BLUE or GREEN for more RED, and squid will likely look for profitable RED or BLUE acquisitions. However, each still guards against trades that the calculator flags as losses, and earlier rejections will make them avoid too lopsided proposals.

By Round 2, both owl and squid have successfully acquired extra RED from others, and iguana has concentrated more into GREEN (and somewhat diminished positions in RED/BLUE). The pattern of accepted trades makes it clearer to all that RED is especially prized by owl and squid, and that iguana is more willing to give up RED/BLUE if compensated sufficiently. With only three turns left, players face a closing-window effect: they may accept slightly smaller gains than before just to capture some surplus before the game ends, but still avoid outright losses. Iguana may seek one last improvement by offloading remaining low-valued color; owl may aim to fine-tune her RED position with small adjustments in GREEN or BLUE; squid may attempt a final opportunistic RED or BLUE acquisition, but if others’ calculators show minimal or negative gains, they will still reject.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with equal chip quantities (10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN) but have different private values for RED and BLUE. GREEN is common-value at $0.50 to everyone, so it acts as a natural numeraire and low-friction currency. Gorilla values RED most ($0.80) and BLUE least ($0.50), Llama values BLUE most ($0.80) and RED at $0.60, and Whale values both RED ($0.60) and BLUE ($0.30) relatively low. This creates strong bilateral gains from trade: Gorilla should want to trade away BLUE for RED; Llama should want to trade away RED for BLUE; Whale should be happy to trade BLUE or RED for GREEN or to exploit the higher valuations of Gorilla and Llama. However, each player only knows their own valuations and the public range ($0.10–$1.00), so they face uncertainty about others’ exact values and must infer them from proposals. With three players and simultaneous responses, each proposal risks multi-acceptance, where the partner is chosen randomly. Proposers must design offers that are individually profitable against multiple possible counterpart valuations while also being attractive enough to get at least one acceptance. Risk attitudes matter: Llama is more risk-seeking and may post aggressive but still-positive offers, Gorilla is more cautious and may aim for clearly safe surplus, and Whale is sophisticated and numerate enough to structure proposals that leverage GREEN as a side-payment resource. Over three short rounds, players will likely focus on obviously beneficial, simple trades (mostly 1–4 chip exchanges between RED/BLUE/GREEN) that generate clear private surplus without requiring fine inference about others’ exact values. The forecast below assumes that no one ever accepts a strictly negative-surplus trade (as revealed by their calculator), some trades are left on the table due to underestimation of others’ values, and GREEN is frequently used as a balancing chip to make offers attractive to at least one counterpart.

rounds.round_explanation: In the initial round, no one has yet observed any trades, so beliefs about others’ values are close to the uninformed prior from the public range. Gorilla, who dislikes risk, will likely start with a conservative, clearly-profitable offer that tries to convert lower-valued BLUE into higher-valued RED, perhaps sweetening with GREEN if needed. Llama, more risk-tolerant, may quickly try to acquire high-value BLUE and is willing to risk slightly thinner margins to get an early deal. Whale, with strong numeracy and moderate risk tolerance, will likely test the market using GREEN as a currency to probe others’ willingness to part with their high-value colors. Players’ responses reflect only their own calculators: they accept when the trade yields positive surplus in their own values and otherwise decline.

By the start of round 1, players have some evidence about others’ preferences. Gorilla and Llama have just completed a mutually beneficial trade, revealing to each other that the other is willing to exchange BLUE and RED at fairly generous rates. Llama then traded with Whale, learning that Whale is also willing to move RED for BLUE at a mild loss in Whale’s own terms, which Llama may interpret as Whale valuing BLUE more than RED (even though Whale actually values BLUE less). These signals encourage Gorilla and Llama to keep targeting each other for favorable RED/BLUE rebalancing, while Whale may try to correct course and use GREEN or small exchanges to extract surplus after observing that others are keen on BLUE and RED. Risk attitudes surface more: Llama may push slightly more aggressive ratios, Gorilla remains selective, and Whale attempts more carefully calibrated proposals after seeing a declined GREEN-for-BLUE test.

In the final round, players know there are only three proposal opportunities left, which reduces the option value of waiting for future trades. Gorilla has already improved her portfolio toward high-value RED and is willing to accept small additional gains if they are clear and risk-free. Llama has been an active trader and is motivated to secure any remaining profitable BLUE-focused deals before the game ends, but must now respect that others have updated beliefs and may not accept thin offers. Whale, after some experimentation, recognizes that GREEN trades at close to parity and that others seem to value RED and BLUE more than he does; he now seeks any safe, incremental swaps that raise his total value, even if the gains are modest. Time pressure leads to slightly more generous final offers and a focus on sure-thing surplus rather than exploratory probing.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with 10 of each chip color and know only their own red/blue values. Green is common-value at $0.50 each. Duck and Tiger value red at $1.00 while Lion values red at $0.90. Duck values blue at $1.00, Lion at $0.50, and Tiger at $0.30. This creates strong gains from trade in blue chips from Lion and Tiger toward Duck (who values blue most), and moderate incentive for Duck and Tiger to buy red from Lion if they think Lion has relatively lower red value. However, because each player sees only their own values, they must infer others’ valuations from behavior during the three-round game. Everyone is risk- and uncertainty-averse to some degree, so they are unlikely to accept any trade that shows a clear loss in their surplus calculator and will favor small, clearly positive trades over risky multi-chip deals. The random matching rule when multiple players accept makes it costly to post deals where more than one partner would find a big gain: a proposer cannot target the highest-surplus counterparty, so they will tend to shape offers such that at most one counterpart has a noticeably attractive payoff, or at least such that either counterpart is acceptable. Given the short horizon (3 rounds × 3 proposals = 9 turns), players have limited time to learn about others’ values and will often test small trades early, then expand slightly if they see acceptance. With high empathy/cooperation tendencies but explicit payoff-maximization instructions, behavior is expected to be individually rational with a slight willingness to leave some surplus to the other side to secure agreement quickly. Across the game, we expect: (1) Duck frequently proposes to buy blue (valuable to her) with red, offering modest but clearly positive margins to others; (2) Lion experiments with red/blue trades but, due to moderate numeracy and lower blue value, tends to sell blue and occasionally buy red when it looks cheap; (3) Tiger, very numerate and high in financial literacy, is aggressive about dumping low-value blue for high-value red, but cautious about overpaying. Because the calculator makes net gains explicit, most accepted trades will be small win–win deals, while any trade that gives a counterparty a loss is immediately rejected. Over three rounds, a few mutually beneficial red-for-blue deals are likely to execute, mostly moving blue toward Duck and red toward Tiger and Lion when prices look attractive, with green chips rarely traded because they are common-value and generate no clear arbitrage.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, no one has seen any behavior yet, so beliefs about others’ values are diffuse. All players know the public value range for red and blue ($0.10–$1.00) and the exact $0.50 value for green, but only their own precise red/blue values. Early proposals are likely to be conservative and exploratory, using small quantities to test appetite for exchanging red and blue. Because everyone is uncertainty-averse, they will gravitate toward trades that show a clear positive gain and avoid zero-sum-looking deals. Green is unlikely to appear in very early proposals, since it is known to be common-value and does not create obvious arbitrage opportunities, but might be used sparingly if someone wants a neutral currency to probe others’ willingness to trade. Each acceptance or rejection will feed into updated beliefs about where other players’ values might lie, especially about whether they place higher or lower value on red or blue.

By round 1, some informative trades have executed. From Duck’s perspective, Lion was willing to give up blue for red, suggesting Lion does not value blue as highly as Duck does and possibly values red somewhat more than blue. Lion has learned that both Duck and Tiger are willing to trade red against blue at modest rates, revealing that at least one of them likely has higher blue value or lower red value than he does. Tiger, after trading with Lion, has confirmed that his own blue is relatively low value and that red is desirable; he has also seen that Duck declined his initial offer, hinting Duck may require better terms for giving up red or that Duck values red and blue similarly. Round 1 is where players start refining offers: Duck can aim more firmly at buying blue with red, Lion may try to balance his red/blue holdings while avoiding clear losses, and Tiger, armed with strong numeracy, will seek higher surplus red-for-blue trades while remaining careful not to show losses on his calculator. The risk of multiple acceptances still weighs on proposers, so they will craft offers that are clearly good for at most one counterpart given what they have inferred so far.

Going into the final round, substantial information about relative valuations has been revealed through repeated red–blue trades. Duck has consistently bought blue from Lion using red, strongly suggesting to both that Duck’s blue valuation is high relative to Lion’s. Tiger has sold blue multiple times for red to Lion, and Lion has been a net buyer of red from Tiger while selling blue, indicating Lion’s effective marginal valuation for red is closer to Duck’s than to Tiger’s but still low enough that these trades clear. Everyone now faces a finite horizon: only three proposals remain in the game. This encourages locking in any remaining clearly positive trades rather than experimenting with new structures. We expect: (a) Duck to seek one more small blue purchase with red from Lion, (b) Lion to be more cautious after seeing his red holdings fall relative to blue and may only accept trades that unambiguously increase his surplus, and (c) Tiger to seize any remaining opportunity to offload cheap blue for valuable red but to avoid overpaying now that the game is nearly over. Because chip holdings have shifted due to earlier trades, proposers must respect inventory constraints when deciding quantities. Final-round proposals will aim to capture the last incremental gains-from-trade without risking obvious losses.

parsed_target.game_explanation: This three-player bargaining game centers on exchanging red, blue, and green chips whose values differ across players for red and blue but are common for green. Green is worth $0.50 to everyone and thus acts as a natural medium of exchange and focal reference: any trade that transforms red/blue into green clarifies value. However, because everyone already holds many green chips at the same value, most surplus comes from exploiting the differences in red vs. blue valuations. Bear values blue highly and red poorly (BLUE $1.00, RED $0.20), Goose values both red and blue well (RED $0.80, BLUE $0.80), and Ram values both moderately (RED $0.50, BLUE $0.80). Bear has the strongest preference to accumulate blue and shed red; Goose is especially hungry for red while also liking blue; Ram sits in the middle, happy to acquire either red or blue at favorable prices. Given simultaneous, private accept/decline decisions and random selection when multiple players accept, proposers must design offers that appeal to at least one counterparty without being excessively generous. Because each player only knows their own values, there is strategic uncertainty: they must infer others’ valuations from what trades are offered and accepted. Early in the game, players test the market with simple one-color-for-one-color swaps or red/blue vs. green offers near break-even according to their own values. The surplus calculator lets everyone see whether a given trade is personally profitable, so irrational acceptance of negative-value trades is unlikely. Successful deals will typically move red from Bear and Ram toward Goose, and blue from Goose and Ram toward Bear, often using green as a balancing chip. Over three rounds, players update beliefs from observed behavior: generous acceptance of blue suggests that player values blue; reluctance to surrender red suggests red is valuable to them. However, with only three rounds and one proposal per player each round, information remains incomplete, and not all potential gains from trade are realized. The likely path is a mix of small, safe surplus-positive trades and a few declined proposals that were either too one-sided or misaligned with others’ priorities.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start, everyone begins with symmetric holdings (10 of each color) but different private values. No one has observed any trades yet, so beliefs about others’ valuations are diffuse. This first round is primarily about exploration: proposers are likely to test simple exchanges that are clearly profitable for themselves but not obviously outrageous, often involving green as a neutral benchmark. Given uncertainty and medium–high risk aversion for Goose and Ram, they will accept only clearly positive deals. Bear, older and numerate but with only moderate cognitive reflection, may open with a cautious, slightly favorable offer. Goose, comparatively cooperative and empathetic, may offer a moderate surplus to entice acceptance while probing how others value red. Ram, highly numerate and strategic, will attempt more precise, value-based offers that secure nontrivial surplus while learning who is willing to exchange which colors.

By round 1, players have seen one successful trade and two failed attempts. The accepted trade from round 0 revealed that Goose is willing to give up blue for red at a rate favorable to Bear, suggesting Goose values red strongly and is flexible with blue. The declined offers involving green on both Goose’s and Ram’s turns suggest that neither Bear nor Goose wants to part with red or blue for green at the proposed terms, hinting that green is not especially attractive as a target asset at those prices. As a result, Bear may double down on trading away red for blue, especially targeting Goose. Goose, having just gained red and lost blue, will try to rebalance while still exploiting her strong value for red, possibly making offers that move surplus via red but must be careful not to overpay with blue or green. Ram, having seen both counterparts reject his green-for-blue test, will pivot toward more direct red–blue exchanges, aiming to exploit his moderate valuations and to benefit from being the marginal trading partner when Bear and Goose’s interests clash.

Entering the final round, all players have more information: Bear has twice succeeded converting red into blue; Goose has shown willingness to pay for red and to trade green for blue; Ram has executed trades both for blue and for red, indicating flexible, moderate valuations. With only one proposal left each, players will favor clearly profitable, relatively small trades that are likely to be accepted. Bear will continue to offer red for blue, but may slightly sweeten the terms given that his remaining red is less urgently unwanted and that others’ holdings have shifted. Goose, having accumulated red and adjusted her blue and green, might seek a last incremental improvement, such as using green to get more blue or modestly trading red for blue if attractive. Ram, now holding a mix of assets adjusted by prior trades, will attempt one last arbitrage, likely using green or his moderately valued red to secure blue or red where it still yields clear surplus. Risk aversion and end-of-game timing mean that egregiously one-sided offers will be rejected; only modest, mutually beneficial-looking trades are likely to go through.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players begin with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN. GREEN is worth $0.50 to everyone, so it behaves like money. RED and BLUE are private-value: buffalo-purple-5232 (buffalo) values RED highly ($0.90) and BLUE low ($0.10); cat-yellow-2069 (cat) values BLUE somewhat more ($0.20) and RED moderately ($0.40); duck-green-2458 (duck) values BLUE very highly ($0.90) and RED low ($0.40). Because players only know their own payoffs, they must infer others’ preferences from observed offers and acceptances. Baseline incentives: buffalo wants to turn low-value BLUE into high-value RED or at least neutral GREEN; duck wants to turn RED into BLUE or GREEN and will often pay for BLUE; cat is in the middle, potentially arbitraging between buffalo’s love of RED and duck’s love of BLUE, but must be careful not to lose value on trades that only shift chips around. Uncertainty arises from not knowing exactly who values what, the random selection when multiple partners accept, and the limited 3-round horizon (9 proposal turns total). Because proposals are evaluated with an on-screen surplus calculator, no one should knowingly accept negative-value trades; instead, bargaining frictions come from mismatched beliefs about what others will accept, the need to offer at least weakly beneficial terms to a potential partner, and the risk that a more preferred partner does not get selected when multiple players accept the same deal. Over the full game we expect early, more exploratory trades as players test how others respond to RED-for-BLUE or GREEN-for-color proposals, followed by more targeted surplus-sharing deals once relative valuations are clearer. Given their personas, duck is likely to be numerically sharp and opportunistic in identifying positive-surplus trades, buffalo somewhat cooperative but cautious about downside and anchor-influenced by obvious win–win offers, and cat somewhat patient but less aggressive in search, accepting clear gains when they appear. The resulting sequence is a plausible, consistent forecast where all accepted trades are individually rational given players’ own values, and proposals gradually reveal that buffalo loves RED, duck loves BLUE, and GREEN is the main numeraire used to compensate trading partners.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game, all players hold identical chip bundles but only know their own values. Round 0 is primarily about discovery. Buffalo will likely start by probing demand for RED, which is very valuable to her, offering GREEN (safe common-value currency) or cheap BLUE. Cat, with moderate valuations, may experiment with small GREEN-for-color trades to see who bites, trying to avoid any obvious value losses. Duck, numerate and strongly favoring BLUE, will look to cheaply acquire BLUE or turn RED into BLUE, but at first will not know that buffalo is a natural RED buyer and that cat is a modest BLUE buyer. Early trades will thus be relatively small and symmetric-looking, as players test each other's willingness to trade GREEN for colored chips and vice versa.

After round 0, some information is revealed. Buffalo has shown she is willing to pay full GREEN value for RED, hinting that RED is valuable to her. Cat has revealed some willingness to buy BLUE with GREEN and to sell RED for BLUE, indicating BLUE is more attractive to her than RED at the margin. Duck has revealed a willingness to swap RED for BLUE, making it likely that BLUE is his most valuable color. By round 1, each player has a rough picture: buffalo likes RED, duck likes BLUE, and cat is in between. Strategies now shift from pure exploration to more targeted deals that exploit these complementarities, with GREEN used to buffer and share surplus. Trades will often turn on cat’s intermediate position: she can profitably sell BLUE to duck and buy RED from buffalo, effectively arbitraging between the extremes while keeping her own value non-decreasing.

By round 2, trading patterns are clear. Buffalo has consistently moved toward RED and away from BLUE, duck toward BLUE and away from RED, and cat has tended to be the intermediary, converting between colored chips and GREEN. Everyone now has fairly strong beliefs about each other's preferences, reducing the need for risky experiments. The remaining opportunities are mostly fine-tuning: buffalo may want a last RED from cat or duck if it is still cheap relative to BLUE, duck may want any remaining BLUE that cat will sell at a GREEN discount, and cat will accept only trades that clearly increase or at least do not reduce her total value. With the end of the game looming, players are less willing to leave money on the table, but also have limited time to negotiate multi-step sequences, so they favor simple, clearly beneficial, one-shot deals. Surplus is extracted where overlapping interests remain; otherwise, marginal or zero-gain proposals are declined.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player game features heterogeneous private values for red and blue chips and a common value for green chips. Everyone starts with the same endowment (10 of each color), but the marginal dollar value of red and blue differs by person: butterfly values red more than blue (0.30 vs 0.10), kangaroo values red especially highly and blue moderately (0.70 vs 0.20), and squid values blue very highly and red moderately (0.80 vs 0.40). Green is worth 0.50 to all three players. Because all players see only their own values, they can calculate their own surplus from any proposed trade but must infer others' values from observed behavior. The strongest structural incentives are: (1) kangaroo wants to acquire red and is comparatively willing to give up blue, (2) squid wants to acquire blue and is comparatively willing to give up red, and (3) butterfly is a weaker red-preferrer than kangaroo and a weaker blue-preferrer than squid, making butterfly a potential middleman using green and moderate-value colors to arbitrage small gains.

Mutually beneficial trades exist: for example, kangaroo giving squid blue in exchange for red can be strongly positive-sum if the rate per chip is not too unfavorable. However, uncertainty about others' valuations, combined with the risk that a too-generous-looking proposal gets snapped up by the "wrong" counterparty (because acceptance is simultaneous and partner selection is random if multiple accept), pushes proposers toward more conservative, self-favorable offers. Since players only get three rounds and one proposal each per round, they face a tension between experimentation (probing others' valuations) and securing sure small gains. High uncertainty aversion and reasonably good numeracy across all three participants suggest they avoid sure personal losses and reject trades that are negative in their own calculator, but will sometimes accept modestly positive trades even if they suspect the proposer benefits more. Over the course of the game, we should see initial exploratory offers that are somewhat unappealing to others, followed by adjustment once players learn who really values which chip colors from the pattern of acceptances and rejections.

Given the personas, butterfly is numerate, risk- and uncertainty-averse, and fairly cooperative, so she will likely propose trades that give herself a clear gain and hope others still see them as worthwhile. Kangaroo is analytical, willing to search broadly, and especially motivated to convert blue into red when possible, but will be careful not to sacrifice green. Squid, with the highest blue value and strong cooperative orientation, is the most likely to propose clean efficiency-enhancing trades involving red-for-blue swaps and to accept slightly unbalanced offers that still give her a clear gain. Overall, the most natural trade pattern is a kangaroo–squid red–blue exchange, potentially with butterfly occasionally using green to take advantage of small valuation gaps as a middleman. We forecast a game where the clearest surplus opportunities between kangaroo and squid are realized by mid-game once their preferences are partially revealed through responses, while some early butterfly proposals are declined as too one-sided or misaligned with others' private values.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of round 0, all players hold 10 of each color and know only their own values. They have no hard evidence yet about others' valuations, so proposals are likely exploratory and slightly self-serving. Butterfly moves first and will probably test whether someone is willing to trade away green or blue at a rate that is modestly favorable for her. Kangaroo, moving second, is strongly motivated to acquire red but does not yet know who values it least; she may try a red-for-blue or red-for-green ask at a rate that she finds attractive, risking rejection. Squid, moving third, places a very high value on blue and moderate value on red, so once she sees kangaroo's and butterfly's first offers and responses, she can begin to infer that there is a good arbitrage opportunity in red–blue trades and may propose something that benefits both her and kangaroo.

By the start of round 1, the only executed trade so far is kangaroo giving up 5 blue to squid for 3 red. This reveals some structure: kangaroo clearly values red and is willing to pay with blue; squid clearly values blue enough to accept this rate. Butterfly, who did not accept that trade, can infer that both others likely value red more than she does or that the terms were not attractive to her private values. Now holdings are: butterfly (10R, 10B, 10G), kangaroo (13R, 5B, 10G), squid (7R, 15B, 10G). Butterfly still has the symmetric endowment and may now position herself as a green or moderate-color middleman, trying to create a trade where she profits while slightly improving someone else’s portfolio. Kangaroo will likely continue to seek red but may target butterfly, who still has 10 red, while being more cautious about giving too much blue away to squid who clearly treasures it. Squid wants more blue only at very favorable rates now that she already has 15; she may consider giving up some red or even green to rebalance if she can secure blue cheaply, or she may experiment with revealing her willingness to trade red for blue more explicitly.

Entering round 2, kangaroo and squid have executed two red–blue trades: in total kangaroo has exchanged 8 blue for 5 red (5-for-3 and 3-for-2), while butterfly has not traded at all. Current holdings now reflect kangaroo’s strong red preference and squid’s strong blue preference: butterfly remains at (10R,10B,10G), kangaroo is at (15R,2B,10G), and squid is at (5R,18B,10G). These realized trades strongly signal to everyone that kangaroo wants red and squid wants blue. Butterfly, seeing this, might attempt a late trade to capture some surplus by offering green or her relatively low-value blue to one of them in exchange for the counterpart color they now have in excess. However, with only one round left, each player is reluctant to risk a small loss, and kangaroo and squid may now insist on very favorable terms. Kangaroo might still try for a final red acquisition from butterfly using her remaining blue, while squid might try a last attempt at getting more blue or shedding some red for blue if it is cheap. But because kangaroo has little blue left and butterfly is cautious, many remaining proposals are likely to be declined as too aggressive or too late to substantially improve others’ payoffs.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players start with 10 RED, 10 BLUE, and 10 GREEN, with GREEN being common-value at $0.50 for everyone and RED/BLUE differing by player. From each player's private perspective, their own high-value color is natural to hoard and their low-value color is natural to trade away, but they do not know how the others rank the chips. The on-screen surplus calculator makes it very easy to avoid trades that are individually negative, so almost all executed trades should be weakly beneficial in dollar terms for both sides. However, because recipients must accept based on their own calculator and multiple acceptances are resolved randomly, senders face important uncertainty: they don't know others' exact values, and they cannot target a single partner when more than one player is willing to accept. This leads to fairly conservative, small proposals that test others' willingness to trade and avoid large, risky offers that might be overly generous to someone whose values they have misinferred. Structurally, BAT has medium BLUE, low RED, and medium GREEN and will be inclined to acquire BLUE or GREEN with RED, but likely starts quite cautiously until they infer others' preferences. FOX has very high BLUE, medium RED, and medium GREEN and thus strongly wants to accumulate BLUE and is more willing to pay with RED or GREEN, but only when the calculator shows a clear gain. FROG values RED the most, GREEN medium, and BLUE lowest, so he is inclined to dump BLUE for RED or GREEN. Over the three rounds, players observe what is accepted, and gradually infer that FOX likes BLUE, FROG likes RED, and BAT seems relatively indifferent between BLUE and GREEN versus RED. Nonetheless, with just three rounds and three turns per round, there is limited time for elaborate signaling: most trades that actually execute will be simple bilateral rebalancing (e.g., FROG giving up BLUE to gain RED, or BAT trading RED away for BLUE/GREEN) where both sides realize small but positive gains, and some turns will end in declined offers as senders test too aggressive or poorly matched ratios. The random partner selection in multi-accept cases further discourages extremely favorable offers targeted at one specific inferred type, since the proposer cannot control which of the inferred-compatible partners they actually trade with.

rounds.round_explanation: At the very start, no one has seen any trade behavior, so they only know their own valuations and the public ranges. Risk-averse, numerate players are likely to propose small, nearly neutral trades to probe the market. BAT knows RED is relatively weak for her and BLUE/GREEN are better, but she has no clear signal on which colors others favor. FOX strongly values BLUE and will avoid parting with it, but might experiment with trading GREEN or some RED. FROG highly values RED and dislikes BLUE, so he is predisposed to trade BLUE away, but he still doesn't know how others rank colors. This round is dominated by exploratory offers and small, low-stakes exchanges, with a fair chance that at least some proposals fail because they accidentally make the responder worse off according to their own calculator.

By the start of round 1, the history already reveals some structure: FOX accepted a 1 RED for 1 BLUE trade as a responder, signaling that she values BLUE at least as much as RED; FROG accepted being paid in RED for BLUE as well, confirming that he likes RED more than BLUE. FOX and FROG have even executed a BLUE-for-RED trade when FROG proposed, which both sides' calculators clearly showed as profitable. BAT has seen that the other two readily trade BLUE and RED in opposite directions, indicating a potential mediated exchange pattern where FOX gives RED for BLUE while FROG gives BLUE for RED. BAT now infers that her low-value RED is likely in relatively high demand by at least one other player and that BLUE is somewhat valuable to both of them, while BLUE is clearly most valuable to FOX and RED most valuable to FROG. Proposals in this round are more targeted: BAT tries to move more RED in exchange for BLUE, FOX leans into trading away RED to acquire BLUE from BAT or FROG, and FROG looks for further chances to dump BLUE for RED. Some proposals still fail when they push ratios too far against a particular responder's calculator.

Going into the final round, a clear bilateral pattern has emerged: FOX and FROG repeatedly accept trades that shift BLUE toward FOX and RED toward FROG, and BAT has successfully sold some RED to FROG for BLUE. Everyone now has reasonably strong beliefs that FOX highly values BLUE, FROG highly values RED, and BAT is willing to trade RED away for BLUE at moderate rates. With only three proposal turns left, each player is trying to capture any remaining easy surplus without overpaying. BAT will look for one last modestly profitable chance to convert low-value RED into BLUE, but must ensure the calculator shows a gain and that at least one counterpart sees a non-negative change. FOX, seeing that both others are cautious but willing to part with BLUE only at favorable terms to themselves, may try a slightly aggressive offer that ends up too unfavorable for the others' calculators and fails. FROG, having profited consistently by selling BLUE for RED, is inclined to make one more such offer to FOX, who has been the more responsive counterparty, aiming for a proposal that FOX's calculator still shows as clearly beneficial. The final trades, if accepted, are small, safe, and consistent with the already-learned valuation pattern.
