parsed_target.game_explanation: All players begin with symmetric chip holdings (10 RED, 10 BLUE, 10 GREEN) but slightly asymmetric private values: Badger and Peacock both value RED at $0.30, BLUE at $0.10, and GREEN at $0.50; Unicorn values RED at $0.50, BLUE at $0.10, and GREEN at $0.50. GREEN is common-value and already the highest value for everyone, so nobody has an incentive to trade GREEN away unless they get more than enough RED in return. The main efficient reallocation margin is moving RED chips from Badger and Peacock (who value them at $0.30) toward Unicorn (who values them at $0.50). BLUE is low-value for everyone ($0.10), so BLUE tends to be the natural “currency” used to pay for surplus-increasing trades or to decorate offers so they look less lopsided. However, since all players know only their own valuations, they are uncertain about others’ values and about how attractive a given trade is to each counterpart. Proposers see clearly whether a trade is profitable for themselves via the on-screen surplus calculator but must guess whether others will accept. Responders see exactly their own gain or loss and will typically reject any proposal giving them negative net value. With high cooperation and empathy priors, players are inclined to make slightly generous, clearly profitable offers to attract acceptance, but they also avoid very risky trades that might lose value if misjudged. Across three rounds, Badger—who is uncertainty-averse—tends to start with conservative, small, value-preserving experiments; Peacock, who is highly numerate and strategic, looks for balanced trades that plausibly make sense given symmetric-looking starting conditions; Unicorn, who most values RED and is highly cooperative but also risk-tolerant in gains, often accepts good RED-acquiring deals and sometimes proposes multi-chip swaps that marginally help others while clearly boosting his own total RED holdings. Over time, some RED is expected to flow toward Unicorn, while BLUE often flows away from Unicorn or sits idle, and GREEN is traded only when the exchange rate is generous enough for both sides to see a clear gain according to their calculators.

rounds.round_explanation: At the outset, no one has seen any trade behavior, and all players only know their own valuations. The most natural early experiments are low-stakes trades that are weakly profitable or value-neutral for the proposer and plausibly appealing to at least one responder under many possible valuation profiles. Badger, who is uncertainty-averse, is likely to propose a small, obviously beneficial trade from her perspective using a highly valued chip as the thing she wants to buy and a lower-valued chip as payment. Peacock, with very strong numeracy and reasoning, will treat the first round as information-gathering but still insist on a positive expected surplus. Unicorn, knowing he values RED highly, will already suspect he might be willing to pay a bit more for RED than others and may test that edge.

After round 0, one successful trade has occurred: Badger bought GREEN from Peacock using BLUE, and the other two proposals were declined. This reveals to all players that at least one counterpart is willing to part with some GREEN for enough BLUE, and that attempts to buy RED only with BLUE at modest exchange rates are unattractive. Badger and Peacock now know that BLUE can sometimes purchase GREEN, but that BLUE alone is not enough to attract RED sellers at the earlier prices. Unicorn has just learned that neither Badger nor Peacock is willing to sell RED for BLUE at a roughly 1.5:1 rate, which hints that they may also value RED fairly highly. In this round, proposers adjust: Badger becomes a bit bolder, Peacock experiments with combined RED–GREEN trade terms, and Unicorn tries mixing RED-for-BLUE with a small GREEN component to make trades look more balanced.

Going into the final round, a pattern has emerged: Badger has successfully converted some BLUE into GREEN and later sold a little RED to Unicorn for BLUE; Peacock has managed a profitable RED-for-GREEN swap with Unicorn; and Unicorn has already started accumulating RED overall, reflecting his higher value for RED. Everyone now has concrete evidence that trades involving RED for BLUE can work if the RED seller is clearly compensated, and that GREEN-for-BLUE trades occur only at exchange rates that keep both sides at least weakly better off. With only one round left, proposers are more willing to make clearly generous, final attempts to secure their preferred portfolios rather than holding out for perfect terms. Badger is inclined to offload remaining low-value BLUE if possible; Peacock, who values RED and GREEN equally, looks for any small last improvement or liquidating BLUE; Unicorn tries one more time to acquire RED on terms that still leave counterparties with non-negative value.

parsed_target.game_explanation: All three players value GREEN equally at $0.50, so GREEN chips are neutral benchmarks. The main gains from trade come from moving RED chips away from mouse (who values them at $0.20) toward dog ($0.80) and, secondarily, owl ($0.30), and moving BLUE chips away from mouse ($0.60) toward dog and owl (both $1.00). Because everyone sees only their own values, each player knows that BLUE is their own best color and GREEN is middling, but they are uncertain about how others rank RED versus BLUE, and how extreme those values are. Dog sees BLUE > RED > GREEN with fairly high BLUE and RED, so she wants to acquire BLUE cheaply (likely from mouse) and avoid giving up too many BLUE. Mouse sees BLUE > GREEN > RED but with a weak value for RED, so efficient deals would have him export RED and maybe some BLUE in exchange for GREEN or small concessions in better colors. Owl sees BLUE > GREEN > RED with strong BLUE and weak RED, similar shape to dog but slightly weaker RED; he is a natural trading partner for mouse on BLUE/RED trades and can also sometimes compete with dog for mouse’s offers. Strategic tension arises because (1) offers must name only one chip color to buy and one to sell, (2) responders decide simultaneously, and (3) if multiple players accept, the partner is chosen randomly. Proposers cannot target a specific partner with certainty, so they must design offers that are attractive enough to at least one counterpart while not being so attractive that an unintended partner both accepts and makes the deal less beneficial than intended. Over three rounds, the most likely pattern is: early, relatively simple and slightly generous offers (especially from the more uncertainty-averse dog) probing what others might accept; then, mouse, with good numeracy and high cooperation orientation, responding to visible opportunities to export his low-value RED or trade some BLUE for GREEN when it still leaves him a surplus; and owl searching actively for favorable BLUE-for-RED or BLUE-for-GREEN deals, sometimes accepting modest but safe gains to avoid ending the game with purely initial holdings. Because there are only nine proposal turns and everyone is moderately uncertainty averse, they are unlikely to hold out for perfectly efficient trades; instead, we should see a small number of conservative, win–win exchanges, mostly involving mouse offloading RED in exchange for GREEN, and possibly dog or owl paying with a few BLUE when the exchange is still net-positive for them.

rounds.round_explanation: At the start of the game all players hold 10 of each chip type and have only their own value schedules. No one yet knows how others rank RED versus BLUE. Dog moves first and, being uncertainty averse but cooperative, is likely to propose a small, low-risk trade that she believes is modestly beneficial to her and plausibly acceptable to someone else. Mouse, observing dog’s initial offer and responses, can begin inferring rough bounds on others’ values and will likely try to offload some of his low-value RED if he can earn a safe gain in GREEN or BLUE. Owl, with high search willingness and strong financial literacy, will look for opportunities to make clear positive-sum trades but is unlikely to accept anything that yields only tiny gains this early, since there are still many turns left.

By the second round, some structure is visible. Dog has already shown a willingness to give up GREEN for BLUE, which signals to others that she values BLUE at least as much as GREEN, and possibly significantly more. Mouse has demonstrated that he is happy to export RED in exchange for GREEN, revealing that RED is relatively weak for him. Owl, having just had a mildly favorable BLUE-for-RED offer rejected by both counterparties, updates that mouse is not easily parted from BLUE unless he receives very good consideration, and that dog is protective of her BLUE. Now, players begin to fine-tune: dog may again use GREEN as a payment medium to get more BLUE but must be careful not to demand too much, or offers will be declined; mouse continues to look for safe ways to dump RED; owl, still searching actively, may try slightly more generous or more targeted trades, particularly with mouse, to secure BLUE while offloading his low-value RED.

In the final round, there are only three proposal turns left, so players can no longer count on future opportunities. Mouse has succeeded in moving a substantial number of his low-value RED to owl in exchange for GREEN and modest gains, while dog has improved her BLUE position slightly at the expense of GREEN and some RED. Owl now holds more RED and somewhat fewer GREEN and BLUE, but each accepted trade has still left him slightly better off given his valuations. Because this is the last chance to improve payoffs, players become more willing to accept small, safe gains they might previously have declined. Dog may propose one more cautious BLUE-for-GREEN or BLUE-for-RED trade, mouse will likely continue trying to shed any remaining excess RED, and owl will selectively accept offers that clearly increase his total value, even if the margin is small. However, the remaining distribution and everyone’s now-updated beliefs also make it harder to find large mutual gains, so several late offers may be declined if they ask for too much or offer too little.

parsed_target.game_explanation:
This three-player bargaining game features private values for red and blue chips and a common value for green chips. All players start with 10 of each color. Because green is worth $0.50 to everyone, it functions as a natural benchmark: trades that simply swap green for other colors tend to be zero- or low-risk in value terms. The real bargaining friction comes from red and blue chips, whose private values differ:

- alligator-orange-6315 (Alligator): RED=$0.60, BLUE=$0.50, GREEN=$0.50. Red is Alligator’s best color, but only slightly above green and blue.
- buffalo-purple-4069 (Buffalo): RED=$0.50, BLUE=$0.20, GREEN=$0.50. Blue is strongly disfavored; red and green are tied and attractive.
- rabbit-yellow-6681 (Rabbit): RED=$1.00, BLUE=$0.30, GREEN=$0.50. Red is extremely valuable; blue is weak; green is moderate.

Even though players know only their own values, some qualitative structure is common knowledge: ranges for chip prices and the fact that others may value colors differently. Rational responders will accept any proposal that strictly increases their own total dollar value and reject proposals that reduce it. They are indifferent at exactly zero surplus, but in practice most people avoid zero-gain deals unless there is some strategic or signaling motive.

The main cross-player complementarities likely to shape trade are:
- Rabbit values red far more than anyone else, so it is efficient for red chips to flow gradually toward Rabbit.
- Buffalo strongly dislikes blue relative to green and red, so Buffalo is eager to dump blue in exchange for green or red.
- Alligator is relatively balanced, slightly favoring red, so can profit by arbitraging between Buffalo’s weak blue and someone else’s stronger blue value, or by moving some red to Rabbit if compensated with multiple blue.

Uncertainty arises because each player is unsure of others’ exact values and must infer them from which deals are offered and accepted. Over only three rounds (nine proposal turns), players have limited time to explore, so they tend to propose clearly self-profitable trades that they expect at least one partner might plausibly accept based on broad ranges: red is typically at the high end of the 0.10–1.00 band, blue at the low-to-mid, green fixed at 0.50. Since acceptance is simultaneous, proposers must design deals that are attractive to at least one counterpart but also robust to partner uncertainty: if both accept, the partner is chosen at random.

Given the short horizon and the surplus calculator, most accepted trades will be modest but strictly positive-sum for both sides, with Buffalo offloading blue for red/green, Rabbit gradually acquiring red, and Alligator capturing small arbitrage gains across colors. Declined offers will mostly be those that accidentally ask a high-valuer to give up its best color too cheaply or that offer blue to Buffalo without enough compensation. The following forecast traces one internally consistent realized path of such bargaining dynamics over the three rounds.

rounds.round_explanation:
At the start of Round 0, all players hold 10 of each color. No one has direct evidence of others’ valuations yet, so proposals will likely be conservative, focusing on trades that look clearly good from the proposer’s own perspective and plausibly acceptable to at least one partner given the public value ranges. We can expect:
- Alligator to test exchanging some of its slightly less-preferred blue for more favored red, but being careful not to give up too much red.
- Buffalo to try to unload low-value blue in exchange for red or green.
- Rabbit to look for opportunities to acquire red while shedding blue, but Rabbit may start cautiously, not wanting to give up too much green too quickly.
Early responses will be informative: whose offers get accepted will hint at where others place their own color priorities.

By the start of Round 1, some structure is revealed:
- From Turn 0, Rabbit accepted 2 BLUE for 1 RED, signaling that Rabbit likely values red more and blue less than Alligator expected; Alligator infers Rabbit is a strong red buyer.
- Buffalo’s blue-for-green offer in Turn 1 was rejected by both, hinting that others do not see green as inferior to blue and may value blue similarly or less than green.
- From Turn 2, Buffalo willingly traded 2 RED for 3 BLUE, indicating Buffalo does not prize red particularly highly relative to blue (or at least is okay swapping some red for extra chips of another color). Rabbit’s acceptance confirms Rabbit is happy to pay in blue to acquire red.
Holdings entering Round 1 are approximately:
- Alligator: RED 11, BLUE 8, GREEN 10.
- Buffalo: RED 8, BLUE 13, GREEN 10.
- Rabbit: RED 11, BLUE 9, GREEN 10.
Now each player adjusts strategy:
- Alligator can profit by selling red to Rabbit for blue or possibly green, knowing Rabbit is a strong red buyer.
- Buffalo knows blue is not attractive to others at 2-for-1 against green, so must sweeten blue offers or instead trade red to Rabbit for green or blue.
- Rabbit continues to chase red but will be more disciplined about how much blue is spent, possibly using green as a payment medium if needed.

Going into Round 2, patterns are clearer:
- Rabbit consistently accepts trades that increase red holdings even at the cost of blue or green, implying a very high private red value. Rabbit has also just been willing to trade GREEN for RED at roughly 1:1 ratio, which suggests red is well above 0.50 for Rabbit.
- Buffalo has now successfully swapped blue for green at a 3:1 ratio, indicating Buffalo strongly prefers green (and red) over blue and is willing to take sizable blue losses in exchange for safer colors.
- Alligator has repeatedly earned small gains by supplying red to Rabbit and getting blue in return, consistent with moderate red preference and willingness to arbitrage.
Approximate holdings after Round 1:
- Alligator: after selling another red for 2 blue in Round 1: RED 10, BLUE 10, GREEN 10.
- Buffalo: after giving Rabbit 3 BLUE for 1 GREEN and then giving Rabbit 2 RED for 2 GREEN: RED 6, BLUE 10, GREEN 11.
- Rabbit: after these trades: RED 13, BLUE 8, GREEN 9.
In the final round, each player knows there will be no future opportunities, so they focus on immediate surplus maximization:
- Alligator may still sell a bit more red to Rabbit if compensated with blue or green, but may not want to go too low in red if values are only slightly higher than others.
- Buffalo prioritizes dumping remaining low-value blue, preferably for green, but must ensure the other side gains too.
- Rabbit, with very high red demand and now quite a lot of red already, may still accept attractive red-buying deals but will avoid trades that sacrifice too much green surplus. The last proposals will tend to be high-surplus, clearly mutually beneficial deals if any such opportunities remain.
